TC-213.description: Check the app's behavior when switching between approximate and precise location permissions at the OS level. Ensure the app responds correctly to these changes and adjusts its functionality accordingly.
TC-213.preconditions:
1. The app is installed and set up on a device with GPS capability.
2. The user has previously granted precise location permission to the app.
3. The app is closed or in the background.
4. The device's location services are enabled.
5. The device OS supports switching between approximate and precise location permissions.
TC-213.title: Switch between approximate and precise location permission

steps.step: Open the device's Settings app and navigate to the app's permissions
steps.result: The app's permission settings are displayed, showing the current location permission status (precise)

steps.step: Change the location permission from precise to approximate
steps.result: The location permission is successfully changed to approximate at the OS level

steps.step: Open the app and attempt to use a location-based feature
steps.result:
1. The app detects the change to approximate location
2. The app treats this as location not given and shows appropriate flows or prompts for precise location access

steps.step: Close the app and return to the device's Settings
steps.result: The Settings app opens successfully

steps.step: Change the location permission back to precise
steps.result: The location permission is successfully changed back to precise at the OS level

steps.step: Open the app again and use a location-based feature
steps.result:
1. The app detects the change to precise location
2. The app resumes full location-based functionality without additional prompts

steps.step: While the app is running, switch between approximate and precise location permissions multiple times
steps.result: The app responds correctly to these changes in real-time, adjusting its functionality accordingly without crashing or showing inconsistent behavior

steps.step: Restart the app after multiple permission switches
steps.result: The app correctly identifies the current permission state (approximate or precise) and behaves consistently with that state

TC-214.description: Confirm that the app correctly remembers and applies the user's location permission choice across app restarts. Ensure that granted permissions remain effective and denied permissions don't result in repeated prompts.
TC-214.preconditions:
1. The app is installed on the device.
2. The user has completed the initial setup (language selection, mobile number entry, and OTP verification).
3. The device has location services available.
TC-214.title: Verify location permission state persistence across app restarts

steps.step: Launch the app and complete the signup process.
steps.result: The app should launch successfully and the user should be able to complete the signup process.

steps.step: When prompted, grant location permission to the app.
steps.result: The app should receive location permission and proceed to the next step in the flow (GPS permission or notification permission, depending on the variant).

steps.step: Complete the rest of the permission flow (GPS and notification permissions).
steps.result: The app should successfully capture all required permissions and assign a store to the user.

steps.step: Force close the app.
steps.result: The app should close without any errors.

steps.step: Relaunch the app.
steps.result: The app should launch without prompting for location permission again. It should remember the previously granted permission.

steps.step: Navigate to a section of the app that uses location services.
steps.result: The app should be able to access the device's location without requesting permission again.

steps.step: Force close the app again.
steps.result: The app should close without any errors.

steps.step: Go to device settings and revoke location permission for the app.
steps.result: The location permission for the app should be successfully revoked.

steps.step: Relaunch the app.
steps.result: The app should launch and detect that location permission has been revoked.

steps.step: Navigate to a section of the app that uses location services.
steps.result: The app should prompt the user to grant location permission again or show the context page with options to 'Allow Access' or 'Select Address Manually', depending on the app variant.

TC-215.description: Validate that the location permission state is correctly maintained after an app update. Ensure that users aren't unnecessarily re-prompted for permissions they've already granted or denied.
TC-215.preconditions:
1. The app is installed on the device with an older version.
2. The user has completed the initial setup and granted or denied location permissions.
3. A new version of the app is available for update.
4. The device has location services available.
TC-215.title: Test location permission flow after app update

steps.step: Launch the current version of the app and note the location permission status.
steps.result: The app should launch and the current location permission status (granted or denied) should be observed.

steps.step: Close the app and update it to the latest version.
steps.result: The app should update successfully without any errors.

steps.step: Launch the updated version of the app.
steps.result: The app should launch without prompting for location permission if it was previously granted. If previously denied, it should not immediately request permission again.

steps.step: Navigate through the app, particularly to sections that use location services.
steps.result:
If permission was previously granted: The app should access location services without requesting permission again.
If permission was previously denied: The app should either show the context page or prompt for permission based on the specific variant flow.

steps.step: If location permission was previously granted, go to device settings and revoke the permission.
steps.result: The location permission for the app should be successfully revoked.

steps.step: Relaunch the updated app after revoking permission.
steps.result: The app should detect the revoked permission and handle it according to its variant flow (e.g., showing context page or prompting for permission when needed).

steps.step: If location permission was previously denied, navigate to a feature requiring location.
steps.result: The app should prompt for location permission or show the context page, respecting the previous denial but offering the option to change.

steps.step: Grant location permission if prompted.
steps.result: The app should successfully capture the location permission and proceed with the appropriate flow.

steps.step: Force close the app and relaunch.
steps.result: The app should remember the newly granted permission and not prompt again.

steps.step: Check if the app handles approximate vs precise location settings correctly after the update.
steps.result: The app should treat approximate location as location not given, and precise location as granted, maintaining this behavior from the previous version.

TC-201.description: Verify the complete permission flow for a fresh app install where the user grants all permissions (location, GPS, and notification) in sequence. This test case covers the critical path of user onboarding and ensures that all permission prompts appear in the correct order and that the app proceeds to the appropriate next step after each permission is granted.
TC-201.preconditions:
1. The app is not installed on the device.
2. The device has location services enabled.
3. The device has GPS capability.
4. The device has an active internet connection.
TC-201.title: Fresh install app launch with all permissions granted

steps.step: Install the app on the device
steps.result: The app is successfully installed

steps.step: Launch the app for the first time
steps.result: The app opens and displays the language selection screen

steps.step: Select a language and proceed
steps.result: The app moves to the mobile number entry screen

steps.step: Enter a valid mobile number and request OTP
steps.result: The OTP is sent to the provided mobile number

steps.step: Enter the correct OTP
steps.result: The app verifies the OTP and completes the signup process

steps.step: When prompted, grant location permission
steps.result: The app receives location permission and proceeds to the next permission request

steps.step: When prompted, grant GPS permission
steps.result: The app receives GPS permission and proceeds to the next permission request

steps.step: When prompted, grant notification permission
steps.result: The app receives notification permission

steps.step: Observe the next screen after granting all permissions
steps.result: The app proceeds to assign a store to the user and completes the onboarding process

TC-202.description: Test the app's behavior when location permission is denied during initial launch. Verify that the app shows the context page with options to 'Allow Access' or 'Select Address Manually'. Ensure the location marker animation plays correctly and that selecting 'Allow Access' re-prompts for location permission.
TC-202.preconditions:
1. The app is not installed on the device.
2. The device has location services enabled.
3. The device has an active internet connection.
TC-202.title: Fresh install app launch with location permission denied

steps.step: Install the app on the device
steps.result: The app is successfully installed

steps.step: Launch the app for the first time
steps.result: The app opens and displays the language selection screen

steps.step: Complete the language selection, mobile number entry, and OTP verification
steps.result: The app completes the signup process

steps.step: When prompted for location permission, deny the permission
steps.result: The app recognizes the denied permission and displays the context page

steps.step: Observe the context page
steps.result: The page shows options to 'Allow Access' or 'Select Address Manually'

steps.step: Watch the location marker on the context page
steps.result: The location marker animates by moving up and down two times

steps.step: Select the 'Allow Access' option
steps.result: The app re-prompts for location permission

steps.step: Grant the location permission this time
steps.result: The app receives location permission and proceeds with the next steps in the permission flow

TC-204.description: Check the app's behavior when notification permission is denied after granting location and GPS permissions. Ensure the app completes the onboarding process correctly and functions properly without notification access.
TC-204.preconditions:
1. The app is not installed on the device.
2. The device has location services enabled.
3. The device has GPS capability.
4. The device has an active internet connection.
TC-204.title: Fresh install app launch with notification permission denied

steps.step: Install the app on the device
steps.result: The app is successfully installed

steps.step: Launch the app and complete the initial setup (language selection, mobile number entry, OTP verification)
steps.result: The app completes the signup process

steps.step: When prompted, grant location permission
steps.result: The app receives location permission

steps.step: When prompted, grant GPS permission
steps.result: The app receives GPS permission

steps.step: When prompted for notification permission, deny the permission
steps.result: The app recognizes the denied notification permission

steps.step: Observe the app's next action
steps.result: The app completes the onboarding process without notification access

steps.step: Navigate through various sections of the app
steps.result: The app functions properly, but does not show any notification-related features

steps.step: Attempt to enable notifications from within the app settings (if available)
steps.result: The app should provide a way to request notification permission again or direct to system settings

TC-205.description: Confirm that the language selection screen appears before any permission requests during fresh install. Ensure that changing the language does not affect the subsequent permission flow.
TC-205.preconditions:
1. The app is not installed on the device.
2. The device has an active internet connection.
3. The device is set to a language different from the app's default language.
TC-205.title: Verify language selection precedes permission requests

steps.step: Install the app on the device
steps.result: The app is successfully installed

steps.step: Launch the app for the first time
steps.result: The app opens and displays the language selection screen

steps.step: Verify that no permission requests have been made yet
steps.result: No permission dialogs have appeared

steps.step: Select a language different from the device's current language
steps.result: The app applies the selected language

steps.step: Proceed to the next screen
steps.result: The app moves to the mobile number entry screen in the selected language

steps.step: Complete the mobile number entry and OTP verification
steps.result: The app verifies the OTP and completes the signup process

steps.step: Observe the sequence of permission requests
steps.result: The app starts requesting permissions in the order: location, GPS, notifications

steps.step: Grant each permission as requested
steps.result: The app receives all permissions and completes the onboarding process

steps.step: Verify that all app content is displayed in the selected language
steps.result: All text and prompts in the app are shown in the language chosen during setup

TC-206.description: Verify that the OTP verification process is completed successfully before the location permission is requested. Ensure that failing OTP verification does not trigger the permission flow prematurely.
TC-206.preconditions:
1. The app is freshly installed on the device.
2. The user has completed the language selection screen.
3. The user has entered a valid mobile number.
4. The OTP has been sent to the user's mobile number.
TC-206.title: Validate OTP verification before location permission

steps.step: Enter an invalid OTP
steps.result: The app should display an error message indicating that the OTP is invalid. The location permission prompt should not appear.

steps.step: Enter the correct OTP
steps.result: The app should verify the OTP successfully and proceed to the next step in the signup process.

steps.step: Complete the signup process
steps.result: The app should finish the signup process without requesting any permissions yet.

steps.step: Observe the next prompt
steps.result: The app should now prompt for location permission, following the Current State flow.

steps.step: Kill the app before granting location permission
steps.result: The app should close without crashing.

steps.step: Relaunch the app
steps.result: The app should resume at the location permission prompt, not asking for OTP verification again.

TC-207.description: Confirm that store assignment occurs only after all permissions (location, GPS, and notification) have been handled, regardless of whether they were granted or denied.
TC-207.preconditions:
1. The app is freshly installed on the device.
2. The user has completed the language selection, mobile number entry, and OTP verification.
3. The signup process is completed.
TC-207.title: Check store assignment after all permissions granted

steps.step: When prompted, grant location permission
steps.result: The app should proceed to the next permission request (GPS or notification, depending on GPS status).

steps.step: If prompted, grant GPS permission
steps.result: The app should proceed to the notification permission request.

steps.step: Grant notification permission
steps.result: The app should proceed to store assignment.

steps.step: Observe the app's next action
steps.result: The app should assign a store to the user.

steps.step: Restart the app
steps.result: The app should open directly to the assigned store, not requesting permissions again.

steps.step: Repeat the process, but deny location permission
steps.result: The app should still proceed through GPS (if applicable) and notification permissions before assigning a store, albeit with limited functionality due to lack of location access.

TC-208.description: Test the app's behavior when device location services are disabled at the system level. Ensure appropriate prompts or instructions are provided to enable location services before proceeding with the permission flow.
TC-208.preconditions:
1. The app is freshly installed on the device.
2. The user has completed the language selection, mobile number entry, and OTP verification.
3. The signup process is completed.
4. Device location services are disabled at the system level.
TC-208.title: Verify permission flow with device location services disabled

steps.step: Observe the app's behavior after signup completion
steps.result: The app should detect that device location services are disabled and show the location bottom sheet with both upper and lower dialogs visible.

steps.step: Tap the 'Enable' button in the upper dialog
steps.result: The app should trigger the system settings for enabling location services.

steps.step: Enable location services in system settings and return to the app
steps.result: The app should detect that location services are now enabled and update the UI accordingly. The upper dialog should disappear, leaving only the lower dialog visible.

steps.step: Tap 'Use Current Location' in the lower dialog
steps.result: The app should now prompt for location permission.

steps.step: Grant location permission
steps.result: The app should proceed to the next step in the permission flow (GPS or notification, depending on GPS status).

steps.step: Complete the remaining permission requests
steps.result: The app should finish the permission flow and assign a store.

steps.step: Disable location services again and restart the app
steps.result: The app should detect the disabled location services and show the appropriate UI (location bottom sheet with both dialogs) without crashing or getting stuck in an incorrect state.

TC-197.description: Verify the system's accuracy in tracking rider return times and enforcing the GT + 5 mins boundary condition for marking out riders. Ensure that riders are correctly marked out if they fail to return within the specified time frame, but not prematurely.
TC-197.preconditions:
1. The system is set up and operational.
2. A rider is registered in the system and currently marked as 'out for delivery'.
3. The Guaranteed Time (GT) for the delivery is defined in the system.
4. The system's clock is accurately synchronized.
5. GPS tracking for the rider is enabled and functioning.
TC-197.title: Precise Rider Return Time Tracking

steps.step: Set up a test delivery with a known Guaranteed Time (GT).
steps.result: The delivery is created in the system with the specified GT.

steps.step: Monitor the rider's status as they approach the GT.
steps.result: The system accurately tracks the rider's location and time.

steps.step: Allow the rider to return exactly at GT + 4 minutes and 59 seconds.
steps.result: The rider's status remains 'out for delivery' and is not marked out.

steps.step: Set up another test delivery with the same GT.
steps.result: A new delivery is created in the system.

steps.step: Allow the rider to return at GT + 5 minutes and 1 second.
steps.result: The system immediately marks the rider as 'out' due to exceeding the GT + 5 mins boundary.

steps.step: Attempt to assign a new order to the marked out rider.
steps.result: The system prevents assignment of new orders to the marked out rider.

steps.step: Manually mark the rider back in.
steps.result: The rider's status is updated to 'available' in the system.

steps.step: Repeat steps 1-3, but simulate GPS signal loss just before GT + 5 mins.
steps.result: The system handles GPS signal loss gracefully and doesn't incorrectly mark out the rider.

steps.step: Restore GPS signal after GT + 5 mins and 30 seconds.
steps.result: The system marks out the rider upon receiving the updated location and time data.

TC-198.description: Test the system's timing precision in enforcing the 3-minute timeout for order acceptance by riders. Verify that riders are accurately marked out if they don't accept an order within 3 minutes, and that acceptances just before the timeout are correctly processed.
TC-198.preconditions:
1. The system is operational with order assignment functionality.
2. At least one rider is available and marked as 'in'.
3. The system's clock is accurately synchronized.
4. The order acceptance timeout is configured to 3 minutes.
TC-198.title: Order Acceptance Timeout Precision

steps.step: Create a new order in the system ready for assignment.
steps.result: The order is successfully created and queued for assignment.

steps.step: Assign the order to an available rider.
steps.result: The system sends an assignment notification to the rider and starts the 3-minute countdown.

steps.step: Monitor the rider's response time as they approach the 3-minute mark.
steps.result: The system accurately tracks the time elapsed since order assignment.

steps.step: Allow the rider to accept the order at 2 minutes and 59 seconds.
steps.result: The system processes the acceptance and assigns the order to the rider.

steps.step: Create another order and assign it to the same rider.
steps.result: A new order is created and assigned to the rider.

steps.step: Let the 3-minute timeout elapse without any action from the rider.
steps.result: The system immediately marks the rider as 'out' at the 3-minute mark and unassigns the order.

steps.step: Attempt to assign a new order to the marked out rider.
steps.result: The system prevents assignment of new orders to the marked out rider.

steps.step: Manually mark the rider back in.
steps.result: The rider's status is updated to 'available' in the system.

steps.step: Assign an order and simulate network latency causing a delayed acceptance response at 3 minutes and 1 second.
steps.result: The system rejects the delayed acceptance and maintains the rider's 'out' status.

TC-199.description: Evaluate the system's ability to execute cron jobs for order assignment and picker status management at precise intervals. Verify that timing-sensitive operations like marking out pickers or updating order statuses occur at the expected times without delays.
TC-199.preconditions:
1. The system is operational with cron jobs configured for order assignment and picker status management.
2. Multiple pickers are registered in the system with varying statuses (in, out, on break).
3. Several orders are in the system at different stages (pending, in progress, completed).
4. The system's clock is accurately synchronized.
5. Cron job schedules are clearly defined and documented.
TC-199.title: Cron Job Execution Timing Accuracy

steps.step: Monitor the execution of the first cron job for order assignment.
steps.result: The cron job executes at the exact scheduled time, assigning orders to available pickers.

steps.step: Observe the second cron job for updating picker statuses.
steps.result: The cron job runs precisely at its scheduled interval, accurately updating picker statuses based on their current conditions.

steps.step: Track the third cron job for marking out inactive pickers.
steps.result: The cron job executes on time, correctly identifying and marking out pickers who meet the criteria for inactivity.

steps.step: Introduce a high system load just before a scheduled cron job.
steps.result: Despite the high load, the cron job executes at its designated time without significant delay.

steps.step: Simulate a brief network outage during a cron job execution.
steps.result: The system handles the outage gracefully, either completing the job when connectivity is restored or logging the failure for retry.

steps.step: Manually change a picker's status immediately before a status update cron job.
steps.result: The cron job captures and reflects the most recent status change in its execution.

steps.step: Schedule multiple cron jobs to run simultaneously.
steps.result: All jobs execute at their scheduled time without interfering with each other, maintaining system stability.

steps.step: Monitor system logs and performance metrics during and after cron job executions.
steps.result: Logs show precise execution times and performance metrics indicate no significant system degradation during cron runs.

TC-200.description: Assess the timing sensitivity of ETA component calculations, particularly for time-based elements like Pick Delay (PD) and Rider Handover Time (RHT). Ensure that these calculations accurately reflect the current system state and are updated in real-time as conditions change.
TC-200.preconditions:
1. The ETA calculation system is operational with all components (PPT, PD, PRT, RHT, RB) functioning.
2. Test orders are set up in various states (pending, in progress, nearing completion).
3. Riders and pickers are available in the system with different statuses.
4. The system's clock is accurately synchronized.
5. External dependencies (e.g., Google API for PRT) are operational or have fallback mechanisms in place.
TC-200.title: ETA Component Calculation Timing

steps.step: Create a new order and initiate the ETA calculation process.
steps.result: The system calculates initial ETA components (PPT, PD, PRT, RHT, RB) based on current conditions.

steps.step: Monitor Pure Pick Time (PPT) calculation as the number of items in the order changes.
steps.result: PPT updates in real-time, accurately reflecting changes in item count and type (regular vs loose items).

steps.step: Observe Pick Delay (PD) calculation as the ratio of pickers to pending orders fluctuates.
steps.result: PD adjusts immediately to reflect the current picker-to-order ratio and any delay penalties.

steps.step: Track Pure Ride Time (PRT) calculation, alternating between successful API calls and fallback scenarios.
steps.result: PRT updates instantly based on API responses or switches to the default value when the API fails.

steps.step: Monitor Rider Handover Time (RHT) as rider availability and order statuses change.
steps.result: RHT calculations reflect real-time changes in rider status and order conditions.

steps.step: Toggle the 'raining' condition on and off to test Rain Buffer (RB) responsiveness.
steps.result: RB immediately adds or removes the 15-minute buffer as the rain condition changes.

steps.step: Rapidly change multiple conditions affecting different ETA components simultaneously.
steps.result: The system accurately recalculates all affected components and provides an updated overall ETA without delay.

steps.step: Introduce network latency or system load and repeat ETA calculations.
steps.result: ETA calculations remain accurate and timely even under stressed system conditions.

steps.step: Compare calculated ETAs with actual completion times for a series of orders.
steps.result: The system demonstrates high accuracy in ETA predictions across various scenarios.

TC-193.description: Verify the system's ability to detect cache corruption during ETA calculations and initiate the re-caching mechanism. Ensure that the system can recover accurate order data without impacting ongoing operations or causing data loss.
TC-193.preconditions:
1. The order delivery system is operational with all components functioning normally.
2. A set of test orders with various statuses (dispatched, confirmed, ready for shipment) are present in the system.
3. The cache is populated with order data.
4. A mechanism to simulate cache corruption is in place.
5. ETA calculation components (PPT, PD, PRT, RHT, RB) are configured and operational.
6. Monitoring tools are set up to detect cache inconsistencies.
TC-193.title: Cache Corruption Detection and Recovery

steps.step: Simulate a cache corruption event during an ETA calculation process.
steps.result: The system detects the cache corruption and logs the event.

steps.step: Observe the system's immediate response to the cache corruption.
steps.result: The system initiates the re-caching mechanism without interrupting ongoing operations.

steps.step: Monitor the re-caching process.
steps.result: The system successfully rebuilds the corrupted cache data from the primary data source.

steps.step: Attempt to perform ETA calculations for affected orders during the recovery process.
steps.result: The system uses alternative data sources or fallback mechanisms to continue ETA calculations without errors.

steps.step: Verify the accuracy of the recovered cache data.
steps.result: All order data in the cache matches the primary data source after recovery.

steps.step: Check the system logs and monitoring tools.
steps.result: The cache corruption event and recovery process are properly logged and reported.

steps.step: Perform ETA calculations on a sample of orders after cache recovery.
steps.result: ETA calculations are accurate and consistent with pre-corruption results.

steps.step: Verify that no orders were lost or their statuses altered during the recovery process.
steps.result: All orders maintain their correct statuses and data integrity.

TC-194.description: Test the system's fault tolerance when a portion of the cache becomes unavailable or corrupted. Verify that the system can continue functioning with the remaining cache data and gradually rebuild the corrupted portion without affecting order processing or ETA calculations.
TC-194.preconditions:
1. The order delivery system is operational with all components functioning normally.
2. The cache is populated with a diverse set of order data, including various order statuses and ETA components.
3. A mechanism to simulate partial cache failure or corruption is in place.
4. Multiple ongoing operations are in progress (e.g., order processing, ETA calculations, rider/picker assignments).
5. Monitoring tools are set up to track system performance and cache status.
TC-194.title: Partial Cache Failure Handling

steps.step: Simulate a partial cache failure, making a portion of the cache unavailable or corrupted.
steps.result: The system detects the partial cache failure and logs the event.

steps.step: Attempt to access data from the corrupted portion of the cache.
steps.result: The system recognizes the unavailability and switches to alternative data sources or fallback mechanisms.

steps.step: Perform ETA calculations for orders affected by the partial cache failure.
steps.result: ETA calculations continue using available cache data and fallback mechanisms, producing reasonably accurate results.

steps.step: Monitor the system's performance during the partial cache failure.
steps.result: The system maintains operational stability with minimal impact on response times.

steps.step: Observe the cache rebuilding process.
steps.result: The system gradually rebuilds the corrupted portion of the cache without disrupting ongoing operations.

steps.step: Continuously process new orders and update existing ones during the cache recovery.
steps.result: New orders are handled correctly, and updates to existing orders are processed without errors.

steps.step: Verify the consistency of data between the recovered cache and the primary data source.
steps.result: The rebuilt cache portion accurately reflects the data in the primary source.

steps.step: Perform a full system check after cache recovery.
steps.result: All system components, including ETA calculations and order processing, function correctly with the fully recovered cache.

TC-195.description: Evaluate the system's performance in recovering from cache corruption or failure during peak hours with high order volumes. Ensure that the recovery process doesn't significantly impact system responsiveness or the accuracy of ongoing operations.
TC-195.preconditions:
1. The order delivery system is configured to handle peak hour traffic.
2. A large volume of test orders (simulating peak hour load) is present in the system.
3. All system components (PPT, PD, PRT, RHT, RB) are operational and under high load.
4. The cache is populated with a large amount of order data.
5. A mechanism to simulate cache corruption or failure during high load is in place.
6. Performance monitoring tools are set up to track system responsiveness and accuracy.
TC-195.title: Cache Recovery During High System Load

steps.step: Simulate peak hour conditions by processing a high volume of orders simultaneously.
steps.result: The system handles the high load, processing orders and calculating ETAs without significant delays.

steps.step: While under peak load, trigger a cache corruption or failure event.
steps.result: The system detects the cache issue and initiates the recovery process while continuing to handle incoming requests.

steps.step: Monitor system responsiveness during the cache recovery process.
steps.result: The system maintains acceptable response times for critical operations like order processing and ETA calculations.

steps.step: Attempt to place new orders and update existing ones during the recovery process.
steps.result: New orders are accepted and existing orders are updated successfully, with minimal additional latency.

steps.step: Perform ETA calculations for a sample of orders during the recovery process.
steps.result: ETA calculations remain reasonably accurate, using fallback data sources when necessary.

steps.step: Monitor the progress of the cache recovery process.
steps.result: The cache is gradually rebuilt without causing system crashes or significant performance degradation.

steps.step: Once recovery is complete, compare system performance metrics to pre-failure state.
steps.result: System performance returns to normal levels, handling the peak load efficiently.

steps.step: Verify the accuracy of recovered cache data and subsequent operations.
steps.result: All recovered data is consistent with the primary data source, and operations (e.g., ETA calculations) produce correct results.

TC-196.description: Assess the consistency and accuracy of recovered cache data after a corruption event. Verify that all order statuses, picker and rider assignments, and ETA calculations are correct and up-to-date following the recovery process.
TC-196.preconditions:
1. The order delivery system is operational with all components functioning normally.
2. A diverse set of test orders with various statuses is present in the system.
3. Picker and rider assignments are in place for multiple orders.
4. The cache is populated with order data, including ETA components (PPT, PD, PRT, RHT, RB).
5. A mechanism to trigger and recover from cache corruption is available.
6. Tools for verifying data consistency across the system are in place.
TC-196.title: Data Consistency After Cache Recovery

steps.step: Trigger a cache corruption event affecting a significant portion of the cached order data.
steps.result: The system detects the corruption and initiates the recovery process.

steps.step: Allow the system to complete the cache recovery process.
steps.result: The cache is rebuilt using data from the primary data source.

steps.step: Verify the order statuses for a sample of orders that were in different stages (e.g., dispatched, confirmed, ready for shipment) before the corruption.
steps.result: All order statuses are correct and consistent with their pre-corruption state.

steps.step: Check picker assignments for orders that were in the picking stage during the corruption event.
steps.result: Picker assignments are accurately maintained or correctly reassigned if necessary.

steps.step: Verify rider assignments for orders that were out for delivery during the corruption event.
steps.result: Rider assignments are accurately maintained, with correct tracking of rider status and location.

steps.step: Perform ETA calculations for a diverse set of orders, including those affected by the cache corruption.
steps.result: ETA calculations are accurate and consistent with the order's current status and system conditions.

steps.step: Compare the Pure Pick Time (PPT), Pick Delay (PD), Pure Ride Time (PRT), Rider Handover Time (RHT), and Rain Buffer (RB) components for affected orders before and after recovery.
steps.result: All ETA components are correctly recalculated and consistent with the current system state.

steps.step: Process updates to existing orders (e.g., status changes, rider reassignments) and create new orders.
steps.result: The system handles updates and new orders correctly, maintaining data consistency in the recovered cache.

steps.step: Run a full system consistency check comparing cache data with the primary data source.
steps.result: No discrepancies are found between the recovered cache and the primary data source.

TC-190.description: Evaluate the system's performance in tracking picker locations when they are working near the store boundaries. Verify that the system doesn't incorrectly mark pickers as outside the store due to GPS inaccuracies, and test the manual location confirmation feature when GPS is unreliable.
TC-190.preconditions:
1. The system is set up with a defined store geofence boundary.
2. Multiple pickers are registered and active in the system.
3. GPS tracking is enabled and functioning for all pickers.
4. The system's picker mark in/out logic is operational.
5. A test environment is set up to simulate various GPS coordinates.
6. The manual location confirmation feature is implemented and available.
TC-190.title: Picker Location Tracking Near Store Boundaries

steps.step: Configure a picker's GPS to show them inside the store, close to the boundary.
steps.result: The system should recognize the picker as being inside the store.

steps.step: Simulate minor GPS fluctuations that occasionally place the picker just outside the boundary.
steps.result: The system should not immediately mark the picker as outside the store due to these minor fluctuations.

steps.step: Move the simulated GPS location to show the picker consistently outside the store boundary, but very close to it.
steps.result: The system should detect that the picker is outside and initiate the process to mark them out, potentially with a grace period.

steps.step: Simulate GPS signal becoming unreliable or lost while the picker is near the boundary.
steps.result: The system should prompt for manual location confirmation from the picker.

steps.step: Provide manual confirmation that the picker is inside the store when prompted.
steps.result: The system should accept the manual confirmation and keep the picker marked as inside the store.

steps.step: Test with multiple pickers simultaneously working near different parts of the store boundary.
steps.result: The system should accurately track all pickers' locations without false mark-outs due to GPS inaccuracies.

steps.step: Simulate a picker moving in and out of the store frequently (e.g., for smoke breaks or short errands).
steps.result: The system should accurately track these movements and only mark the picker out if they remain outside for longer than the allowed duration.

TC-191.description: Assess the system's ability to handle GPS signal instability for both riders and pickers near geofence boundaries. Verify that the system doesn't trigger false mark-outs or incorrect location statuses due to temporary GPS fluctuations.
TC-191.preconditions:
1. The system is set up with defined store geofence boundaries.
2. Multiple riders and pickers are registered and active in the system.
3. GPS tracking is enabled for all users.
4. The system's mark in/out logic for both riders and pickers is operational.
5. A test environment is set up to simulate various GPS signal conditions.
6. The manual location confirmation feature is available for situations with unreliable GPS.
TC-191.title: Geofence Performance Under GPS Signal Instability

steps.step: Simulate normal GPS signals for riders and pickers near store boundaries.
steps.result: The system should accurately track locations and statuses.

steps.step: Introduce minor GPS signal fluctuations for users near boundaries.
steps.result: The system should not trigger false mark-outs or status changes for minor, temporary fluctuations.

steps.step: Simulate significant GPS jumps (e.g., showing a user suddenly 100m away and then back).
steps.result: The system should have mechanisms to detect and handle these anomalies without immediate status changes.

steps.step: Create a scenario with intermittent GPS signal loss for multiple users.
steps.result: The system should gracefully handle signal loss, potentially prompting for manual confirmation if the duration exceeds a threshold.

steps.step: Simulate GPS drift showing gradual, inaccurate movement across boundaries.
steps.result: The system should have tolerance for gradual drift and not immediately change user statuses.

steps.step: Test with a high volume of users all experiencing various levels of GPS instability simultaneously.
steps.result: The system should maintain accurate tracking and statuses for all users without performance degradation.

steps.step: Introduce GPS spoofing attempts (simulating a user trying to fake their location).
steps.result: The system should have measures to detect potential GPS spoofing and handle it appropriately (e.g., requiring manual confirmation or flagging for review).

TC-192.description: Test the system's performance when conducting geofence validations for multiple riders and pickers simultaneously during peak hours. Ensure that the system can handle the load without delays in status updates or impact on order assignments and ETA calculations.
TC-192.preconditions:
1. The system is set up with defined store geofence boundaries for multiple stores.
2. A large number of riders and pickers are registered and active in the system.
3. GPS tracking is enabled for all users.
4. The system's mark in/out logic, order assignment, and ETA calculation features are fully operational.
5. A test environment is set up to simulate peak hour conditions with high user activity.
6. Performance monitoring tools are in place to measure system responsiveness and accuracy.
TC-192.title: Multiple Geofence Validations During Peak Hours

steps.step: Simulate peak hour conditions with a high volume of riders and pickers active across multiple store locations.
steps.result: The system should handle the increased load without significant delays in processing location data.

steps.step: Trigger simultaneous geofence validations for a large number of users (both riders and pickers) across different stores.
steps.result: The system should accurately validate all geofence positions without errors or timeouts.

steps.step: Introduce a mix of users entering, exiting, and hovering around geofence boundaries simultaneously.
steps.result: The system should correctly update the status of all users based on their geofence positions without delays.

steps.step: Initiate multiple order assignments and ETA calculations while geofence validations are ongoing.
steps.result: Order assignments and ETA calculations should proceed accurately and without significant delay, using the most up-to-date location data.

steps.step: Simulate rapid status changes for multiple users (e.g., riders accepting orders, pickers starting picks) concurrent with geofence validations.
steps.result: The system should handle all status updates correctly, maintaining data consistency and accuracy in geofence positions.

steps.step: Introduce network latency or brief outages during peak geofence validation periods.
steps.result: The system should gracefully handle temporary issues, queuing validations if necessary and catching up without data loss once issues are resolved.

steps.step: Run a full cycle of operations including order creation, assignment, picking, and delivery, with continuous geofence validations throughout.
steps.result: All operations should complete successfully with accurate geofence data incorporated into decisions and calculations.

steps.step: Monitor system performance metrics throughout the peak hour simulation.
steps.result: System should maintain acceptable response times and accuracy levels as defined in the performance requirements, with no significant degradation due to the high volume of geofence validations.

TC-185.description: Test the system's ability to handle multiple riders returning to the store simultaneously. Verify that the system accurately updates rider statuses, manages geofence boundaries, and correctly calculates Rider Handover Time (RHT) for all affected orders.
TC-185.preconditions:
1. The system is operational and able to handle multiple concurrent operations.
2. There are multiple riders (at least 5) currently out for delivery.
3. There are multiple pending orders in the system.
4. The store geofence is properly configured.
5. The Rider Handover Time (RHT) calculation logic is implemented and functioning.
6. The system's GPS tracking for riders is active and accurate.
TC-185.title: Multiple Simultaneous Rider Returns

steps.step: Simulate 5 riders simultaneously returning to the store's geofence boundary.
steps.result: The system should detect all 5 riders entering the store's geofence simultaneously.

steps.step: Verify the system updates the status of all 5 riders.
steps.result: All 5 riders should have their status updated to 'returned' or 'available' in the system.

steps.step: Check the geofence validation for all 5 riders.
steps.result: The system should correctly validate that all 5 riders are within the store's geofence.

steps.step: Trigger the Rider Handover Time (RHT) calculation for pending orders.
steps.result: The system should recalculate RHT for all affected pending orders, considering the newly available riders.

steps.step: Verify the accuracy of RHT calculations for multiple orders.
steps.result: The RHT for pending orders should be correctly updated, reflecting the increased availability of riders.

steps.step: Monitor system performance and response time during this operation.
steps.result: The system should maintain responsiveness and perform all operations without significant delay or errors.

steps.step: Attempt to assign new orders to the returned riders.
steps.result: The system should be able to assign new orders to the returned riders based on the updated availability.

steps.step: Verify the overall ETA calculations for affected orders.
steps.result: The overall ETA for affected orders should be recalculated accurately, incorporating the updated RHT values.

TC-186.description: Evaluate the system's performance when assigning a large number of orders to pickers and riders simultaneously during peak hours. Ensure that the assignment logic works correctly, Pick Delay (PD) and Rider Handover Time (RHT) calculations are accurate, and the system remains responsive.
TC-186.preconditions:
1. The system is operational and configured for peak hour operations.
2. There is a large number of pending orders in the system (at least 50).
3. Multiple pickers (at least 10) and riders (at least 15) are available.
4. The order assignment logic, Pick Delay (PD), and Rider Handover Time (RHT) calculation systems are implemented and functioning.
5. The system is set to simulate peak hour conditions.
TC-186.title: Concurrent Order Assignments During Peak Hours

steps.step: Initiate a bulk order creation process, simulating a sudden influx of 50 new orders.
steps.result: The system should successfully create and register all 50 new orders.

steps.step: Trigger the order assignment process for all pending orders.
steps.result: The system should start assigning orders to available pickers and riders based on the assignment logic.

steps.step: Monitor the Pick Delay (PD) calculation for each order during the assignment process.
steps.result: The system should calculate PD accurately for each order, considering the number of available pickers and pending orders.

steps.step: Verify the Rider Handover Time (RHT) calculation for each order.
steps.result: The system should calculate RHT correctly for each order, taking into account rider availability and order status.

steps.step: Check the overall ETA calculation for all orders.
steps.result: The system should compute accurate ETAs for all orders, incorporating PPT, PD, PRT, RHT, and RB components.

steps.step: Monitor system response time and performance during the bulk assignment process.
steps.result: The system should maintain responsiveness and complete all assignments without significant delays or errors.

steps.step: Verify the final assignment status of all orders.
steps.result: All orders should be correctly assigned to pickers and riders based on the system's assignment logic.

steps.step: Attempt to make real-time updates to some orders (e.g., cancellations, modifications) during the assignment process.
steps.result: The system should handle real-time updates correctly without disrupting the ongoing assignment process.

TC-187.description: Assess the system's capability to handle a large number of riders marking in or out concurrently, such as during shift changes. Verify that all rider statuses are updated correctly, geofence validations are performed accurately, and the impact on order assignments and ETA calculations is managed properly.
TC-187.preconditions:
1. The system is operational and configured to handle rider status changes.
2. A large number of riders (at least 50) are currently in the system.
3. The rider mark in/out functionality is implemented and working.
4. Geofence validation for riders is active and accurate.
5. The system's order assignment and ETA calculation modules are functioning.
6. Some orders are in various stages of the delivery process.
TC-187.title: Mass Rider Mark In/Out Operations

steps.step: Simulate a shift change scenario by initiating mass rider mark-out operations for 25 riders simultaneously.
steps.result: The system should process all 25 rider mark-out requests concurrently and update their statuses correctly.

steps.step: Verify the geofence validation for all riders marking out.
steps.result: The system should accurately perform geofence checks for all riders marking out, ensuring they are at the correct location.

steps.step: Immediately after the mark-out operations, initiate mass rider mark-in operations for 25 new riders.
steps.result: The system should process all 25 rider mark-in requests concurrently and update their statuses to available.

steps.step: Check the geofence validation for all riders marking in.
steps.result: The system should correctly validate the location of all riders marking in, ensuring they are within the store's geofence.

steps.step: Verify the impact on ongoing order assignments.
steps.result: The system should adjust ongoing order assignments based on the new rider availability, without errors or inconsistencies.

steps.step: Trigger ETA recalculations for affected orders.
steps.result: The system should recalculate ETAs for all affected orders, considering the changes in rider availability.

steps.step: Monitor system performance and response time during these mass operations.
steps.result: The system should maintain responsiveness and perform all operations without significant delay or errors.

steps.step: Attempt to assign new orders immediately after the mass mark in/out operations.
steps.result: The system should correctly assign new orders based on the updated rider availability.

steps.step: Verify the accuracy of Rider Handover Time (RHT) calculations after the mass operations.
steps.result: RHT calculations for pending orders should accurately reflect the new rider availability scenario.

TC-188.description: Test the system's performance when recalculating ETAs for a large number of orders simultaneously, such as when weather conditions change (activating or deactivating the Rain Buffer). Ensure that all ETA components (PPT, PD, PRT, RHT, RB) are calculated correctly for each order without system slowdown.
TC-188.preconditions:
1. The system is operational with the ETA calculation module functioning correctly.
2. A large number of active orders (at least 100) are in various stages of the delivery process.
3. The weather condition monitoring system is active and can trigger changes.
4. All ETA components (PPT, PD, PRT, RHT, RB) are implemented and functioning.
5. The system's caching mechanism for order data is operational.
6. Some riders and pickers are available in the system.
TC-188.title: Simultaneous ETA Recalculations for Multiple Orders

steps.step: Simulate a sudden change in weather conditions (e.g., from not raining to raining).
steps.result: The system should detect the weather change and prepare to update the Rain Buffer (RB) for all active orders.

steps.step: Trigger simultaneous ETA recalculations for all active orders (at least 100).
steps.result: The system should initiate ETA recalculations for all affected orders concurrently.

steps.step: Monitor the calculation of Pure Pick Time (PPT) for each order.
steps.result: PPT should be calculated correctly for each order based on its specific characteristics (number of items, loose items, etc.).

steps.step: Verify the Pick Delay (PD) calculations during the mass recalculation.
steps.result: PD should be accurately computed for each order, considering the current picker availability and order queue.

steps.step: Check the Pure Ride Time (PRT) calculations for all orders.
steps.result: PRT should be calculated correctly, either using the Google API (if available) or falling back to the default time.

steps.step: Validate the Rider Handover Time (RHT) calculations for all orders.
steps.result: RHT should be accurately calculated for each order based on the current rider availability and order status.

steps.step: Confirm the application of the Rain Buffer (RB) to all orders.
steps.result: The 15-minute rain buffer should be correctly applied to all orders due to the simulated weather change.

steps.step: Verify the final ETA calculations for all orders.
steps.result: The system should compute the final ETA for each order correctly using the formula: C + max(A+B, D) + F.

steps.step: Monitor system performance and response time during the mass ETA recalculation.
steps.result: The system should maintain responsiveness and complete all ETA recalculations without significant delays or errors.

steps.step: Check the caching mechanism for updated order data.
steps.result: The system should efficiently update and manage the cache for all recalculated order ETAs.

steps.step: Attempt to retrieve updated ETAs for multiple orders simultaneously.
steps.result: The system should provide the newly calculated ETAs for all queried orders quickly and accurately.

TC-181.description: Verify cache consistency when an order rapidly transitions through multiple statuses (e.g., ORDER_CONFIRMED to dispatched to delivered) within a short time frame. Ensure that the cache accurately reflects the current order status at each stage.
TC-181.preconditions:
1. The order delivery system is up and running.
2. The cache management system is active and functioning.
3. There is at least one order in the system with ORDER_CONFIRMED status.
4. A rider is available and marked as 'in' for delivery.
5. A picker is available and marked as 'in' for picking.
6. The system is configured to handle rapid status changes.
TC-181.title: Rapid Order Status Transitions

steps.step: Create a new order with ORDER_CONFIRMED status.
steps.result: The order is successfully created and cached with ORDER_CONFIRMED status.

steps.step: Rapidly transition the order status to 'dispatched' within 5 seconds of creation.
steps.result: The order status is updated to 'dispatched' and the cache reflects this change immediately.

steps.step: Assign a rider to the order and mark it as 'out for delivery' within 5 seconds of dispatch.
steps.result: The order is assigned to a rider, marked as 'out for delivery', and the cache is updated accordingly.

steps.step: Mark the order as 'delivered' within 5 seconds of being out for delivery.
steps.result: The order status is updated to 'delivered' and the cache reflects this final status change.

steps.step: Verify the cache status of the order at each transition point.
steps.result: The cache accurately reflects the current order status at each stage of the rapid transitions.

steps.step: Attempt to calculate the ETA for the order at each status change.
steps.result: The ETA calculation uses the most up-to-date information from the cache at each status change.

steps.step: Check the order's final status in the cache after all transitions.
steps.result: The cache shows the order's final status as 'delivered' with no remnants of previous statuses.

steps.step: Verify that the order is removed from the cache after delivery.
steps.result: The order data is successfully removed from the cache upon delivery status.

TC-182.description: Test the system's ability to maintain cache consistency when multiple orders are being updated simultaneously while ETA calculations are in progress. Verify that the ETA calculations use the most up-to-date order information from the cache.
TC-182.preconditions:
1. The order delivery system is operational with cache management active.
2. Multiple orders (at least 10) are present in the system with various statuses.
3. Sufficient riders and pickers are available and marked as 'in'.
4. The ETA calculation system is ready to process multiple orders simultaneously.
5. The system is configured to handle concurrent updates and calculations.
TC-182.title: Concurrent Order Updates and ETA Calculations

steps.step: Initiate simultaneous status updates for multiple orders (at least 5) to different statuses (e.g., ORDER_CONFIRMED, dispatched, out for delivery, delivered).
steps.result: All order status updates are processed concurrently without errors.

steps.step: Trigger ETA calculations for all orders in the system immediately after initiating the status updates.
steps.result: ETA calculations start for all orders without waiting for status update completions.

steps.step: Monitor the cache updates for each order during the status change process.
steps.result: The cache reflects real-time status changes for each order without conflicts or data loss.

steps.step: Verify the ETA calculation results for each order after completion.
steps.result: All ETA calculations use the most recent order status information from the cache.

steps.step: Rapidly change the status of one order multiple times during ongoing ETA calculations.
steps.result: The system handles the rapid status changes without crashing, and the final ETA calculation uses the latest status.

steps.step: Check the consistency of cache data for all orders after the concurrent operations.
steps.result: The cache data is consistent with the actual order statuses in the database for all orders.

steps.step: Attempt to retrieve cached data for an order that was marked as delivered during the process.
steps.result: The system correctly shows that the order data has been removed from the cache upon delivery.

steps.step: Verify the system logs for any errors or inconsistencies during the concurrent operations.
steps.result: No critical errors or data inconsistencies are reported in the system logs.

TC-183.description: Evaluate the effectiveness of the bulk cache refresh mechanism (ETA_data_refresh cron) when order statuses are changing at a high frequency. Ensure that the refresh operation doesn't interfere with ongoing status updates and maintains data integrity.
TC-183.preconditions:
1. The order delivery system is operational with cache management and ETA_data_refresh cron job active.
2. A large number of orders (at least 100) are present in the system.
3. The system is configured to handle high-frequency status changes.
4. The ETA_data_refresh cron job is scheduled to run at a known interval.
TC-183.title: Cache Refresh During High-Frequency Status Changes

steps.step: Initiate high-frequency status changes for multiple orders (at least 50) at a rate of 1 change per second per order.
steps.result: The system processes all status changes without errors or significant delays.

steps.step: Monitor the cache updates during the high-frequency status changes.
steps.result: The cache reflects all status changes accurately and in real-time.

steps.step: Allow the ETA_data_refresh cron job to run during the high-frequency status changes.
steps.result: The cron job executes successfully without interfering with ongoing status updates.

steps.step: Verify the cache data immediately after the cron job completes.
steps.result: The cache data is consistent with the actual order statuses, including the most recent changes.

steps.step: Attempt to calculate ETAs for multiple orders during the high-frequency status changes and cron job execution.
steps.result: ETA calculations complete successfully using the most up-to-date information from the cache.

steps.step: Rapidly change the status of several orders (at least 10) immediately after the cron job completes.
steps.result: The system handles the new status changes correctly, updating the cache without conflicts from the recent refresh.

steps.step: Check the system logs for any errors, warnings, or performance issues during the high-frequency operations and cron job execution.
steps.result: No critical errors are reported, and performance metrics are within acceptable ranges.

steps.step: Verify the data integrity of all orders in the cache after the high-frequency changes and cron job execution.
steps.result: All order data in the cache matches the corresponding data in the database, with no inconsistencies or data loss.

TC-184.description: Assess cache consistency when an order is cancelled and immediately re-created with the same order ID. Verify that the cache correctly removes the cancelled order data and accurately caches the new order information without any residual data from the cancelled order.
TC-184.preconditions:
1. The order delivery system is operational with cache management active.
2. At least one order is present in the system with a known order ID.
3. The system is configured to handle order cancellations and re-creations.
4. The cache is functioning correctly for order storage and removal.
TC-184.title: Order Cancellation and Re-creation Impact on Cache

steps.step: Create a new order with a specific order ID and ORDER_CONFIRMED status.
steps.result: The order is successfully created and cached with the given order ID.

steps.step: Verify the initial cache state for the created order.
steps.result: The cache contains the correct order information for the given order ID.

steps.step: Cancel the order immediately after creation.
steps.result: The order status is updated to 'cancelled' in the system.

steps.step: Verify the cache state immediately after cancellation.
steps.result: The order data is successfully removed from the cache upon cancellation.

steps.step: Recreate a new order with the same order ID immediately after cancellation.
steps.result: A new order is created with the same order ID and cached successfully.

steps.step: Verify the cache state for the recreated order.
steps.result: The cache contains the new order information without any residual data from the cancelled order.

steps.step: Attempt to calculate the ETA for the recreated order.
steps.result: The ETA calculation uses only the new order data without any influence from the cancelled order.

steps.step: Change the status of the recreated order to 'dispatched'.
steps.result: The order status is updated to 'dispatched' and the cache reflects this change accurately.

steps.step: Check the system logs for any errors or warnings during the cancellation and recreation process.
steps.result: No critical errors or warnings are reported in the system logs related to cache management or data integrity.

steps.step: Verify that no trace of the cancelled order remains in the system or cache.
steps.result: The system and cache contain only the information of the recreated order, with no remnants of the cancelled order.

TC-177.description: Verify the system's ability to execute cron jobs for order assignment and picker status management under high network latency conditions. Ensure that the system gracefully handles delays and maintains data integrity.
TC-177.preconditions:
1. The order delivery system is up and running.
2. Multiple pickers and riders are registered in the system.
3. Several orders are in the system in various states (e.g., pending, confirmed, ready for pickup).
4. A mechanism to simulate high network latency is in place.
5. Cron jobs for order assignment and picker status management are scheduled.
6. Monitoring tools are set up to observe system performance and data integrity.
TC-177.title: Cron Job Execution During High Network Latency

steps.step: Simulate high network latency conditions (e.g., 500ms-1000ms delay).
steps.result: Network latency is successfully simulated without causing system failure.

steps.step: Trigger the cron job for order assignment.
steps.result: The cron job starts execution despite the high latency.

steps.step: Monitor the time taken for the order assignment cron job to complete.
steps.result: The job takes longer to complete due to high latency but finishes without timing out.

steps.step: Verify the status of assigned orders after the cron job completion.
steps.result: All eligible orders are correctly assigned to available pickers without duplication or missing assignments.

steps.step: Trigger the cron job for picker status management.
steps.result: The cron job starts execution despite the high latency.

steps.step: Monitor the time taken for the picker status management cron job to complete.
steps.result: The job takes longer to complete due to high latency but finishes without timing out.

steps.step: Verify the picker statuses after the cron job completion.
steps.result: All picker statuses are correctly updated based on their current conditions (e.g., on break, outside store, actively picking).

steps.step: Check the system logs for any errors or warnings related to the cron job executions.
steps.result: Logs show increased execution times but no critical errors or data inconsistencies.

steps.step: Verify the ETA calculations for orders during this high latency period.
steps.result: ETAs are calculated correctly, accounting for the increased processing time due to latency.

steps.step: Simulate a picker manually marking out during high latency.
steps.result: The system successfully processes the manual mark out, updating the picker's status correctly despite the latency.

steps.step: Verify the cache consistency for orders and picker statuses after all operations.
steps.result: The cache reflects accurate and consistent data for all orders and picker statuses, matching the database state.

steps.step: Gradually reduce the simulated network latency back to normal levels.
steps.result: The system returns to normal performance without requiring a restart or manual intervention.

steps.step: Run a final cron job cycle for both order assignment and picker status management under normal latency.
steps.result: Cron jobs execute at their usual speed, and all data remains consistent with the operations performed during high latency.

TC-178.description: Test the accuracy of ETA calculations when network latency causes delays in receiving Google API responses for Pure Ride Time (PRT) calculation. Verify that the system correctly falls back to the default delivery travel time of 15 minutes when API calls fail due to network issues.
TC-178.preconditions:
1. The ETA calculation system is up and running.
2. Google API for Pure Ride Time (PRT) calculation is configured.
3. Default delivery travel time is set to 15 minutes.
4. Network latency simulation tool is available.
5. Test order data is prepared with known parameters (Pure Pick Time, Pick Delay, etc.).
6. Rain condition is set to False (no rain buffer).
TC-178.title: ETA Calculation Accuracy with Delayed API Responses

steps.step: Create a test order with known parameters for Pure Pick Time (A), Pick Delay (B), and Rider Handover Time (D).
steps.result: Test order is created successfully with known values for A, B, and D components.

steps.step: Simulate normal network conditions and trigger ETA calculation for the test order.
steps.result: ETA calculation is performed successfully using actual Google API response for Pure Ride Time (C).

steps.step: Record the calculated ETA under normal conditions.
steps.result: ETA under normal conditions is recorded for comparison.

steps.step: Simulate high network latency causing delays in Google API responses.
steps.result: Network latency is successfully simulated, causing delays in API responses.

steps.step: Trigger ETA calculation for the same test order under simulated network latency.
steps.result: System attempts to calculate ETA but experiences delays in receiving Google API response.

steps.step: Increase network latency further to cause API call failure.
steps.result: Google API call fails due to excessive network latency.

steps.step: Verify that the system falls back to the default delivery travel time of 15 minutes for Pure Ride Time (C).
steps.result: System correctly uses the default 15 minutes for Pure Ride Time (C) in ETA calculation.

steps.step: Compare the ETA calculated under network latency with the ETA from normal conditions.
steps.result: The difference in ETAs is exactly 15 minutes (default time) minus the original Google API time.

steps.step: Gradually reduce network latency and repeat ETA calculations.
steps.result: System switches back to using Google API responses for Pure Ride Time as latency decreases.

steps.step: Monitor system logs and performance metrics during the test.
steps.result: Logs show appropriate error handling and fallback mechanism activation. Performance metrics indicate increased response time during high latency periods.

TC-179.description: Evaluate the system's ability to manage order caching operations (caching on dispatch, removal on delivery/cancellation, and bulk refresh) during periods of high network latency. Ensure that cache consistency is maintained and no data loss occurs.
TC-179.preconditions:
1. The order caching system is operational.
2. Test orders in various states (dispatched, delivered, cancelled, shipment ready, ORDER_CONFIRMED) are prepared.
3. Network latency simulation tool is available.
4. Bulk cache refresh cron job (ETA_data_refresh) is configured.
5. System monitoring tools are in place to track cache operations and consistency.
TC-179.title: Order Cache Management Under Network Stress

steps.step: Create a set of test orders in different states (dispatched, delivered, cancelled, shipment ready, ORDER_CONFIRMED).
steps.result: Test orders are successfully created and their initial cache status is verified.

steps.step: Simulate normal network conditions and perform standard cache operations (caching on dispatch, removal on delivery/cancellation).
steps.result: Cache operations are performed successfully under normal conditions. Cache consistency is maintained.

steps.step: Introduce moderate network latency and repeat cache operations for a new set of orders.
steps.result: Cache operations are completed with slight delays. No data loss or inconsistency is observed.

steps.step: Increase network latency to high levels and attempt to cache a large batch of orders on dispatch.
steps.result: System experiences delays in caching operations but eventually completes without data loss. Cache consistency is maintained.

steps.step: Under high latency, trigger multiple simultaneous cache operations (e.g., caching new orders while removing delivered orders).
steps.result: System manages concurrent cache operations without conflicts. All operations are eventually completed correctly despite delays.

steps.step: Initiate the bulk cache refresh cron job (ETA_data_refresh) under high network latency conditions.
steps.result: Bulk refresh operation takes longer than usual but completes successfully. Cache data is updated correctly for all relevant orders.

steps.step: Rapidly change the status of several orders (e.g., from dispatched to delivered) while the bulk refresh is in progress.
steps.result: System correctly handles the status changes and updates the cache accordingly, maintaining consistency even with the ongoing bulk refresh.

steps.step: Simulate a network outage during a cache operation and restore connectivity after a short period.
steps.result: System resumes and completes the interrupted cache operation upon network restoration. No data loss occurs.

steps.step: Verify the final cache state for all test orders after the series of operations under varying network conditions.
steps.result: Cache state correctly reflects the final status of all orders. No inconsistencies or data loss is detected.

steps.step: Analyze system logs and performance metrics for the duration of the test.
steps.result: Logs show appropriate handling of network delays and retries. Performance metrics indicate increased operation times during high latency but no failures or data corruption.

TC-180.description: Assess the system's capability to accurately update rider and picker statuses (mark in/out, geofence validation) when experiencing network delays. Verify that the system doesn't incorrectly mark out riders or pickers due to delayed status updates.
TC-180.preconditions:
1. Rider and picker management systems are operational.
2. Test riders and pickers are set up in the system.
3. Geofence boundaries for test stores are configured.
4. Network latency simulation tool is available.
5. GPS simulation tool for riders and pickers is set up.
6. System is configured with timeout values (e.g., 3 minutes for order acceptance, GT + 5 mins for rider return).
TC-180.title: Rider and Picker Status Updates with Network Delays

steps.step: Set up test scenarios with riders and pickers in various initial states (marked in, marked out, on break).
steps.result: Test riders and pickers are successfully set up with known initial states.

steps.step: Simulate normal network conditions and perform standard status update operations (mark in/out, geofence entry/exit).
steps.result: Status updates are processed quickly and accurately under normal conditions.

steps.step: Introduce moderate network latency and attempt rider mark-out when exiting store geofence.
steps.result: Rider status is updated to 'marked out' with a slight delay, but before the system timeout.

steps.step: Increase network latency to high levels and simulate a picker trying to mark in for a shift.
steps.result: Picker mark-in is processed with significant delay, but system does not create duplicate mark-in entries.

steps.step: Under high latency, simulate a rider accepting an order near the 3-minute timeout threshold.
steps.result: System correctly processes the acceptance without marking out the rider, despite the delayed update.

steps.step: Simulate a rider returning from delivery under high network latency, approaching the GT + 5 mins threshold.
steps.result: System accurately tracks the rider's return and doesn't incorrectly mark them out, allowing for the delayed status update.

steps.step: Rapidly move a test rider in and out of store geofence while experiencing network delays.
steps.result: System correctly tracks the rider's location without erroneously marking them out due to delayed updates.

steps.step: Simulate network outage during a picker's attempt to go on break.
steps.result: System queues the status update and applies it correctly once network connectivity is restored.

steps.step: Trigger multiple simultaneous status updates for different riders and pickers under varying levels of network latency.
steps.result: All status updates are eventually processed correctly, maintaining accurate states for all riders and pickers.

steps.step: Verify final status of all test riders and pickers after the series of operations under network stress.
steps.result: All rider and picker statuses accurately reflect their last known state, with no inconsistencies due to network delays.

steps.step: Review system logs and performance metrics for the duration of the test.
steps.result: Logs show appropriate handling of delayed updates and timeout management. Metrics indicate increased processing times but no incorrect status changes due to network issues.

TC-169.description: Confirm that an order is cached when it is dispatched. Create a new order, dispatch it, and verify that it appears in the cache with the correct status and details.
TC-169.preconditions:
1. The order management system is up and running.
2. The cache system is operational and accessible.
3. There is at least one store with available inventory.
4. There is at least one active rider available for dispatch.
TC-169.title: Verify order caching on dispatch

steps.step: Create a new order in the system with status 'ORDER_CONFIRMED'.
steps.result: The order is successfully created in the system with a unique order ID.

steps.step: Verify that the order is not present in the cache immediately after creation.
steps.result: The order is not found in the cache.

steps.step: Dispatch the order to an available rider.
steps.result: The order status changes to 'DISPATCHED' and is assigned to a rider.

steps.step: Check the cache immediately after dispatch.
steps.result: The order appears in the cache with the correct order ID, 'DISPATCHED' status, and all relevant order details.

steps.step: Verify the cached order details.
steps.result: The cached order contains accurate information including order ID, status, items, quantities, store details, and assigned rider information.

steps.step: Update an attribute of the order (e.g., add a note).
steps.result: The order is updated in the system.

steps.step: Check the cache again after the update.
steps.result: The cached order reflects the updated information, confirming that the cache is synchronized with the main database.

TC-170.description: Ensure that an order is removed from the cache when it is marked as delivered. Dispatch an order, mark it as delivered, and verify that it is no longer present in the cache.
TC-170.preconditions:
1. The order management system is operational.
2. The cache system is functioning correctly.
3. There is at least one active order in 'DISPATCHED' status in the cache.
4. A rider is assigned to the dispatched order.
TC-170.title: Validate cache removal on order delivery

steps.step: Select a dispatched order from the cache for testing.
steps.result: A suitable order is identified in the cache with 'DISPATCHED' status.

steps.step: Verify the presence of the selected order in the cache.
steps.result: The order details are confirmed to be present in the cache.

steps.step: Simulate the delivery process by marking the order as 'DELIVERED' in the system.
steps.result: The order status is successfully updated to 'DELIVERED' in the main database.

steps.step: Wait for any potential cache update processes to complete (if asynchronous).
steps.result: Sufficient time has passed for cache updates to propagate.

steps.step: Check the cache for the presence of the delivered order.
steps.result: The order is no longer present in the cache, confirming its removal upon delivery.

steps.step: Attempt to retrieve the delivered order's details from the cache using its order ID.
steps.result: The system returns a null or empty result, indicating the order is not in the cache.
