steps.step: Verify the order details in the main database.
steps.result: The order is still present in the main database with 'DELIVERED' status, confirming that only the cache entry was removed.

TC-171.description: Confirm that an order is removed from the cache when it is cancelled. Create and dispatch an order, cancel it, and verify that it is removed from the cache immediately.
TC-171.preconditions:
1. The order management system is operational.
2. The cache system is functioning correctly.
3. There is at least one active order in 'DISPATCHED' status in the cache.
4. The system allows order cancellation.
TC-171.title: Check cache removal on order cancellation

steps.step: Select a dispatched order from the cache for testing.
steps.result: A suitable order is identified in the cache with 'DISPATCHED' status.

steps.step: Verify the presence of the selected order in the cache.
steps.result: The order details are confirmed to be present in the cache.

steps.step: Initiate the cancellation process for the selected order.
steps.result: The system accepts the cancellation request.

steps.step: Confirm the order cancellation in the main database.
steps.result: The order status is updated to 'CANCELLED' in the main database.

steps.step: Immediately check the cache for the presence of the cancelled order.
steps.result: The order is no longer present in the cache, confirming its immediate removal upon cancellation.

steps.step: Attempt to retrieve the cancelled order's details from the cache using its order ID.
steps.result: The system returns a null or empty result, indicating the order is not in the cache.

steps.step: Verify the order details in the main database.
steps.result: The order is still present in the main database with 'CANCELLED' status, confirming that only the cache entry was removed.

steps.step: Monitor the cache for a short period to ensure the cancelled order doesn't reappear.
steps.result: The cancelled order remains absent from the cache, confirming the permanence of the removal.

TC-172.description: Ensure that an order is cached when its status changes to shipment ready. Create an order, update its status to shipment ready, and confirm it appears in the cache with correct details.
TC-172.preconditions:
1. The order management system is operational.
2. The cache system is functioning correctly.
3. There is at least one store with available inventory.
4. The system supports a 'shipment ready' status for orders.
TC-172.title: Verify order caching on shipment ready status

steps.step: Create a new order in the system with status 'ORDER_CONFIRMED'.
steps.result: The order is successfully created in the system with a unique order ID.

steps.step: Verify that the newly created order is not present in the cache.
steps.result: The order is not found in the cache.

steps.step: Update the order status to 'shipment ready' in the system.
steps.result: The order status is successfully updated to 'shipment ready' in the main database.

steps.step: Check the cache immediately after the status update.
steps.result: The order appears in the cache with the correct order ID, 'shipment ready' status, and all relevant order details.

steps.step: Verify the cached order details.
steps.result: The cached order contains accurate information including order ID, status, items, quantities, and store details.

steps.step: Update an attribute of the 'shipment ready' order (e.g., add a note).
steps.result: The order is updated in the system.

steps.step: Check the cache again after the update.
steps.result: The cached order reflects the updated information, confirming that the cache is synchronized with the main database.

steps.step: Change the order status to a different status (e.g., 'In Transit').
steps.result: The order status is updated in the main database.

steps.step: Verify the cache after the status change.
steps.result: The order remains in the cache with the updated status, confirming that caching persists beyond the 'shipment ready' status.

TC-173.description: Check that an order is cached when it is created or updated with ORDER_CONFIRMED status. Create a new order with ORDER_CONFIRMED status and verify it is added to the cache.
TC-173.preconditions:
1. The order management system is operational.
2. The cache system is functioning correctly.
3. There is at least one store with available inventory.
4. The system supports creating orders with 'ORDER_CONFIRMED' status.
TC-173.title: Validate order caching for ORDER_CONFIRMED status

steps.step: Create a new order in the system with status 'ORDER_CONFIRMED'.
steps.result: The order is successfully created in the system with a unique order ID and 'ORDER_CONFIRMED' status.

steps.step: Check the cache immediately after order creation.
steps.result: The order appears in the cache with the correct order ID, 'ORDER_CONFIRMED' status, and all relevant order details.

steps.step: Verify the cached order details.
steps.result: The cached order contains accurate information including order ID, status, items, quantities, and store details.

steps.step: Update an attribute of the 'ORDER_CONFIRMED' order (e.g., add a customer note).
steps.result: The order is updated in the system.

steps.step: Check the cache again after the update.
steps.result: The cached order reflects the updated information, confirming that the cache is synchronized with the main database.

steps.step: Create another order with a different initial status (e.g., 'PENDING').
steps.result: A new order is created with 'PENDING' status.

steps.step: Verify that the 'PENDING' order is not in the cache.
steps.result: The 'PENDING' order is not found in the cache.

steps.step: Update the 'PENDING' order status to 'ORDER_CONFIRMED'.
steps.result: The order status is successfully updated to 'ORDER_CONFIRMED' in the main database.

steps.step: Check the cache after updating the order to 'ORDER_CONFIRMED'.
steps.result: The previously 'PENDING' order now appears in the cache with 'ORDER_CONFIRMED' status and correct details.

steps.step: Attempt to modify the cached order directly in the cache (if possible).
steps.result: The system prevents direct modification of cached orders, ensuring data integrity.

TC-174.description: Verify cache consistency when an order undergoes rapid status changes. Create an order, quickly transition it through multiple statuses (e.g., confirmed, shipment ready, dispatched, delivered), and ensure the cache accurately reflects each state.
TC-174.preconditions:
1. The order cache management system is up and running.
2. The system is configured to handle rapid status changes.
3. Test data for an order is available.
4. All necessary components (database, caching system, order management system) are operational.
TC-174.title: Test cache consistency during rapid status updates

steps.step: Create a new order in the system with initial status as 'CREATED'.
steps.result: The order is successfully created and cached with 'CREATED' status.

steps.step: Quickly change the order status to 'ORDER_CONFIRMED'.
steps.result: The order status is updated to 'ORDER_CONFIRMED' and the cache reflects this change immediately.

steps.step: Rapidly update the order status to 'SHIPMENT_READY'.
steps.result: The order status is updated to 'SHIPMENT_READY' and the cache is updated accordingly without delay.

steps.step: Quickly change the order status to 'DISPATCHED'.
steps.result: The order status is changed to 'DISPATCHED' and the cache is updated to reflect this status immediately.

steps.step: Rapidly update the order status to 'DELIVERED'.
steps.result: The order status is updated to 'DELIVERED' and the cache accurately reflects this final status.

steps.step: Verify the cache contents for the order after each status change.
steps.result: The cache consistently reflects the correct status of the order at each stage of the rapid updates.

steps.step: Check the system logs for any cache inconsistencies or errors during the rapid status changes.
steps.result: No cache inconsistencies or errors are reported in the system logs.

steps.step: Perform a final verification of the order status in both the database and the cache.
steps.result: The order status in the database and cache match and show 'DELIVERED'.

TC-175.description: Confirm that the cache is refreshed during ETA calculations. Modify an order's details, trigger an ETA calculation, and verify that the cache is updated with the new information.
TC-175.preconditions:
1. The order cache management system is operational.
2. ETA calculation system is functioning correctly.
3. Test order data is available in the system.
4. Cache refresh mechanism during ETA calculations is enabled.
TC-175.title: Assess cache refresh during ETA calculations

steps.step: Create a test order with initial details and cache it.
steps.result: The order is created and cached successfully with initial details.

steps.step: Verify the initial cached data for the order.
steps.result: The cache contains the correct initial order details.

steps.step: Modify the order details (e.g., change the number of items, delivery address).
steps.result: The order details are updated in the database but not yet in the cache.

steps.step: Trigger an ETA calculation for the modified order.
steps.result: The ETA calculation process is initiated successfully.

steps.step: Wait for the ETA calculation to complete.
steps.result: The ETA calculation completes without errors.

steps.step: Check the cached data for the order immediately after ETA calculation.
steps.result: The cache is updated with the new order details and the calculated ETA.

steps.step: Verify that all modified order details are correctly reflected in the cache.
steps.result: All changes made to the order are accurately represented in the updated cache.

steps.step: Trigger another ETA calculation without changing the order details.
steps.result: The ETA calculation process is initiated and completed successfully.

steps.step: Verify that the cache remains consistent and unchanged after the second ETA calculation.
steps.result: The cache data remains the same as it was after the first update, showing no unnecessary changes.

TC-176.description: Test the bulk cache refresh mechanism through the ETA_data_refresh cron job. Modify multiple orders, run the cron job, and verify that all affected orders in the cache are updated correctly.
TC-176.preconditions:
1. The order cache management system is operational.
2. ETA_data_refresh cron job is set up and functional.
3. Multiple test orders are available in the system.
4. The system is configured to perform bulk cache refresh.
TC-176.title: Evaluate bulk cache refresh via ETA_data_refresh cron

steps.step: Create multiple test orders (at least 5) with varying details and cache them.
steps.result: Multiple orders are created and cached successfully.

steps.step: Verify the initial cached data for all created orders.
steps.result: The cache contains correct initial details for all test orders.

steps.step: Modify the details of all test orders (e.g., change statuses, add/remove items, update delivery addresses).
steps.result: The order details are updated in the database but not yet in the cache.

steps.step: Manually trigger the ETA_data_refresh cron job.
steps.result: The ETA_data_refresh cron job starts executing.

steps.step: Monitor the cron job execution and wait for it to complete.
steps.result: The cron job completes its execution without any errors.

steps.step: Check the cached data for all modified orders immediately after cron job completion.
steps.result: The cache is updated with the new details for all modified orders.

steps.step: Verify that all changes made to each order are correctly reflected in the cache.
steps.result: All modifications to the orders are accurately represented in the updated cache.

steps.step: Check the system logs for any errors or inconsistencies during the bulk refresh process.
steps.result: No errors or inconsistencies are reported in the system logs related to the bulk refresh process.

steps.step: Perform a final verification by comparing the database data with the cached data for all test orders.
steps.result: The database data and cached data match perfectly for all test orders, confirming successful bulk refresh.

TC-162.description: Verify the comprehensive ETA calculation using all components: Pure Pick Time (A), Pick Delay (B), Pure Ride Time (C), Rider Handover Time (D), and Rain Buffer (F). Set A=200s, B=100s, C=900s, D=150s, F=900s. Expected ETA should be C + max(A+B, D) + F = 900s + max(300s, 150s) + 900s = 2100s.
TC-162.preconditions:
1. The ETA calculation system is up and running.
2. All components of the ETA calculation are available and functioning correctly.
3. The system is configured with the following values:

steps.step: Confirm the final ETA calculation.

steps.step: Verify that the system logs or records the Google API failure.

steps.step: Retrieve the order details including its payment mode (COD).
steps.result: The system successfully retrieves the COD order details.

steps.step: Verify the assigned rider's status.
steps.result: The rider's status is confirmed as 'out for delivery'.

steps.step: Initiate the Rider Handover Time (RHT) calculation for the order.
steps.result: The system begins calculating the RHT.

steps.step: Check the calculated Rider Handover Time.
steps.result: The system sets the Rider Handover Time to 0 seconds.

steps.step: Ensure that the standard RHT calculation logic (heap of remaining ride time) was not used.
steps.result: The system did not use the standard calculation logic for this COD order with 'out for delivery' status.

steps.step: Record the calculated Rider Handover Time in the order details.
steps.result: The system successfully updates the order details with the 0-second Rider Handover Time.

steps.step: Verify that this 0-second RHT doesn't negatively impact the overall ETA calculation.
steps.result: The overall ETA calculation correctly handles the 0-second RHT without producing erroneous results.

TC-152.description: Determine handover time for an online payment order when the rider is 'out for delivery'. Ensure the handover time is calculated using the standard logic rather than being set to 0 seconds.
TC-152.preconditions:
1. The order delivery system is up and running.
2. An online payment order is in the system.
3. A rider is assigned to the order.
4. The rider's status is set to 'out for delivery'.
5. The Rider Handover Time (RHT) calculation module is functional.
6. A heap of remaining ride times for other orders is available in the system.
TC-152.title: Evaluate handover time for online payment orders with rider out for delivery

steps.step: Retrieve the order details including its payment mode (online payment).
steps.result: The system successfully retrieves the online payment order details.

steps.step: Confirm the assigned rider's status.
steps.result: The rider's status is verified as 'out for delivery'.

steps.step: Initiate the Rider Handover Time (RHT) calculation for the order.
steps.result: The system begins calculating the RHT using the standard logic.

steps.step: Verify that the system uses the heap of remaining ride times for the calculation.
steps.result: The system correctly uses the heap of remaining ride times in the RHT calculation.

steps.step: Calculate the Rider Handover Time based on the standard logic.
steps.result: The system computes the RHT using the heap of remaining ride times, not setting it to 0 seconds.

steps.step: Confirm that the calculated RHT is greater than 0 seconds.
steps.result: The calculated RHT is verified to be greater than 0 seconds, indicating the use of standard logic.

steps.step: Record the calculated Rider Handover Time in the order details.
steps.result: The system successfully updates the order details with the calculated Rider Handover Time.

steps.step: Verify that the RHT calculation considers any delivery_time_exceeded penalties if applicable.
steps.result: The system correctly applies any relevant delivery_time_exceeded penalties in the RHT calculation.

TC-153.description: Calculate handover time for a COD order when the rider has 'reached destination'. Confirm that the handover time is set to 0 seconds as per the specified logic.
TC-153.preconditions:
1. The order delivery system is operational.
2. A Cash on Delivery (COD) order is present in the system.
3. A rider is assigned to the order.
4. The rider's status is set to 'reached destination'.
5. The Rider Handover Time (RHT) calculation module is functional.
TC-153.title: Test handover time for COD orders with rider reached destination

steps.step: Retrieve the order details including its payment mode (COD).
steps.result: The system successfully retrieves the COD order details.

steps.step: Verify the assigned rider's status.
steps.result: The rider's status is confirmed as 'reached destination'.

steps.step: Initiate the Rider Handover Time (RHT) calculation for the order.
steps.result: The system begins calculating the RHT.

steps.step: Check the calculated Rider Handover Time.
steps.result: The system sets the Rider Handover Time to 0 seconds.

steps.step: Ensure that the standard RHT calculation logic (heap of remaining ride time) was not used.
steps.result: The system did not use the standard calculation logic for this COD order with 'reached destination' status.

steps.step: Record the calculated Rider Handover Time in the order details.
steps.result: The system successfully updates the order details with the 0-second Rider Handover Time.

steps.step: Verify that this 0-second RHT is appropriately handled in the overall ETA calculation.

steps.step: Confirm that the system is ready to process the cash payment upon delivery.
steps.result: The system is prepared to handle the cash payment process for this COD order.

TC-154.description: Compute handover time for an online payment order when the rider has 'reached destination'. Validate that the standard handover time calculation is used instead of setting it to 0 seconds.
TC-154.preconditions:
1. The ETA calculation system is up and running.
2. An order with online payment is in the system.
3. A rider is assigned to the order.
4. The rider's status is set to 'reached destination'.
5. The standard handover time calculation logic is implemented.
TC-154.title: Verify handover time for online payment orders with rider reached destination

steps.step: Retrieve the order details for the online payment order.
steps.result: The system successfully retrieves the order details.

steps.step: Check the rider's status for the order.
steps.result: The rider's status is confirmed as 'reached destination'.

steps.step: Trigger the handover time calculation for the order.
steps.result: The system initiates the handover time calculation.

steps.step: Verify that the standard handover time calculation is used.
steps.result: The system uses the standard handover time calculation logic instead of setting it to 0 seconds.

steps.step: Examine the calculated handover time.
steps.result: The handover time is not 0 seconds and reflects the standard calculation based on the current system conditions (e.g., number of riders, order queue, etc.).

steps.step: Compare the calculated handover time with the expected value based on the current system state.
steps.result: The calculated handover time matches the expected value according to the standard calculation logic.

steps.step: Verify that the calculated handover time is included in the overall ETA calculation.
steps.result: The calculated handover time is correctly incorporated into the overall ETA calculation: ETA = Pure ride time (C) + max(Pure pick time (A) + Pick delay (B), Rider handover time (D)) + Rain buffer (F)

TC-155.description: Test the system's ability to recalculate handover time when an order's payment method is changed from COD to online payment (or vice versa) at different stages of delivery. Verify that the handover time is adjusted correctly based on the new payment method and current rider status.
TC-155.preconditions:
1. The ETA calculation system is operational.
2. An order is present in the system.
3. The system allows changing the payment method of an order.
4. A rider is assigned to the order.
5. The handover time calculation logic for both COD and online payment is implemented.
TC-155.title: Assess handover time calculation when switching between COD and online payment

steps.step: Create a new order with COD payment method.
steps.result: A new order is created in the system with COD payment.

steps.step: Calculate and record the initial handover time for the COD order.
steps.result: The system calculates and stores the initial handover time for the COD order.

steps.step: Change the rider's status to 'picked'.
steps.result: The rider's status is updated to 'picked' in the system.

steps.step: Recalculate and record the handover time.
steps.result: The system recalculates the handover time based on the rider's 'picked' status for a COD order.

steps.step: Change the order's payment method from COD to online payment.
steps.result: The system updates the order's payment method to online payment.

steps.step: Trigger handover time recalculation.
steps.result: The system recalculates the handover time based on the new online payment method and the rider's current status.

steps.step: Verify that the handover time has been adjusted correctly for online payment.
steps.result: The new handover time reflects the correct calculation for an online payment order with the rider's current status.

steps.step: Change the rider's status to 'out for delivery'.
steps.result: The rider's status is updated to 'out for delivery' in the system.

steps.step: Recalculate and record the handover time.
steps.result: The system recalculates the handover time based on the rider's 'out for delivery' status for an online payment order.

steps.step: Change the order's payment method back to COD.
steps.result: The system updates the order's payment method back to COD.

steps.step: Trigger handover time recalculation.
steps.result: The system recalculates the handover time based on the COD payment method and the rider's current 'out for delivery' status.

steps.step: Verify that the handover time has been adjusted correctly for COD.
steps.result: The new handover time reflects the correct calculation for a COD order with the rider's current status.

steps.step: Change the rider's status to 'reached destination'.
steps.result: The rider's status is updated to 'reached destination' in the system.

steps.step: Recalculate and record the final handover time for COD.
steps.result: The system recalculates the handover time based on the rider's 'reached destination' status for a COD order.

steps.step: Verify that all handover time calculations are correctly incorporated into the overall ETA calculations throughout the process.
steps.result: All handover time calculations are correctly used in the ETA formula: ETA = Pure ride time (C) + max(Pure pick time (A) + Pick delay (B), Rider handover time (D)) + Rain buffer (F)

TC-143.description: Confirm that the delivery_time_exceeded penalty is added to the handover time calculation when a rider is delayed. Create a scenario where a rider's delivery is running late and verify that the penalty is correctly applied.
TC-143.preconditions:
1. The order delivery system is up and running.
2. A rider is available and assigned to an order.
3. The order is in 'out for delivery' status.
4. The system has a configured delivery_time_exceeded penalty.
TC-143.title: Verify additional handover time for delayed deliveries

steps.step: Set up an order that is running late for delivery.
steps.result: The order status shows as delayed in the system.

steps.step: Calculate the Rider Handover Time (RHT) for this delayed order.
steps.result: The system calculates RHT including the delivery_time_exceeded penalty.

steps.step: Verify the calculated RHT value.
steps.result: The RHT value includes the standard handover time plus the delivery_time_exceeded penalty.

steps.step: Compare this RHT with a non-delayed order's RHT.
steps.result: The delayed order's RHT is higher by exactly the amount of the delivery_time_exceeded penalty.

TC-144.description: Ensure that no additional penalty is added to the handover time for deliveries that are not running late. Create a scenario with on-time deliveries and verify that only the standard handover time (based on rider availability) is applied.
TC-144.preconditions:
1. The order delivery system is up and running.
2. Multiple riders are available and assigned to orders.
3. All orders are in 'out for delivery' status and on time.
TC-144.title: Validate no additional handover time for on-time deliveries

steps.step: Set up multiple orders that are on time for delivery.
steps.result: All orders show as on time in the system.

steps.step: Calculate the Rider Handover Time (RHT) for these on-time orders.
steps.result: The system calculates RHT without any additional penalties.

steps.step: Verify the calculated RHT values for all orders.
steps.result: The RHT values only include the standard handover time based on rider availability, without any additional penalties.

steps.step: Compare these RHT values with each other and with the known standard handover time.
steps.result: All RHT values are consistent and do not include any extra penalties.

TC-145.description: Assess how the system handles multiple delayed deliveries. Create several orders that are running late and verify that the delivery_time_exceeded penalty is applied correctly to each, potentially resulting in a cumulative delay in the handover time calculation.
TC-145.preconditions:
1. The order delivery system is up and running.
2. Multiple riders are available and assigned to orders.
3. Several orders are in 'out for delivery' status and running late.
4. The system has a configured delivery_time_exceeded penalty.
TC-145.title: Test cumulative handover time for multiple delayed deliveries

steps.step: Set up multiple orders that are running late for delivery.
steps.result: Multiple orders show as delayed in the system.

steps.step: Calculate the Rider Handover Time (RHT) for all delayed orders.
steps.result: The system calculates RHT including the delivery_time_exceeded penalty for each delayed order.

steps.step: Verify the calculated RHT values for all delayed orders.
steps.result: Each delayed order's RHT includes the standard handover time plus the delivery_time_exceeded penalty.

steps.step: Calculate the cumulative handover time for all delayed orders.
steps.result: The cumulative handover time reflects the sum of individual RHTs, each including its own penalty.

steps.step: Compare the cumulative handover time with the sum of standard handover times without penalties.
steps.result: The difference equals the total of all applied delivery_time_exceeded penalties.

TC-146.description: Evaluate the system's behavior when a delivery transitions from on-time to delayed. Create an order that is exactly at the threshold of its expected delivery time and observe how the system applies the penalty as it crosses into 'delayed' status.
TC-146.preconditions:
1. The order delivery system is up and running.
2. A rider is available and assigned to an order.
3. The order is in 'out for delivery' status and approaching its expected delivery time.
4. The system has a configured delivery_time_exceeded penalty and a defined threshold for when a delivery becomes 'delayed'.
TC-146.title: Check penalty application at the threshold of becoming delayed

steps.step: Set up an order that is very close to its expected delivery time (e.g., 1 minute before becoming delayed).
steps.result: The order shows as on time in the system.

steps.step: Calculate the Rider Handover Time (RHT) for this order.
steps.result: The system calculates RHT without the delivery_time_exceeded penalty.

steps.step: Wait until the order crosses the threshold and becomes delayed.
steps.result: The order status changes to delayed in the system.

steps.step: Immediately recalculate the RHT for the now-delayed order.
steps.result: The system recalculates RHT, now including the delivery_time_exceeded penalty.

steps.step: Compare the two RHT calculations (before and after becoming delayed).
steps.result: The difference in RHT values exactly matches the delivery_time_exceeded penalty.

TC-147.description: Test the system's ability to remove the additional penalty when a previously delayed delivery becomes on-time (e.g., due to updated ETA calculations or changes in traffic conditions). Ensure the delivery_time_exceeded penalty is removed appropriately.
TC-147.preconditions:
1. The order delivery system is up and running.
2. A rider is available and assigned to an order.
3. The order was previously delayed but conditions have improved (e.g., traffic cleared up).
4. The system has a configured delivery_time_exceeded penalty and the ability to update ETAs based on current conditions.
TC-147.title: Verify penalty removal when delayed delivery becomes on-time

steps.step: Set up an order that is currently delayed and has the delivery_time_exceeded penalty applied.
steps.result: The order shows as delayed in the system with an increased RHT due to the penalty.

steps.step: Update the system conditions to reflect improved delivery times (e.g., simulate cleared traffic).
steps.result: The system recognizes the changed conditions.

steps.step: Trigger a recalculation of the ETA for the delayed order.
steps.result: The system updates the ETA based on the new conditions.

steps.step: Verify the order status after ETA recalculation.
steps.result: The order status changes from delayed to on-time.

steps.step: Recalculate the Rider Handover Time (RHT) for this order.
steps.result: The system recalculates RHT without the delivery_time_exceeded penalty.

steps.step: Compare the new RHT with the previous (delayed) RHT.
steps.result: The new RHT is lower, with the difference exactly matching the removed delivery_time_exceeded penalty.

TC-148.description: Evaluate how the system combines the delivery_time_exceeded penalty for running late deliveries with the maximum handover time of 1800s when no riders are available. Verify that the total handover time does not exceed the maximum allowed time in the system.
TC-148.preconditions:
1. The order delivery system is up and running.
2. There is at least one order in the system that is running late (delivery_time_exceeded).
3. There are no available riders in the system.
4. The delivery_time_exceeded penalty is configured in the system.
5. The maximum handover time of 1800s (30 minutes) for no rider availability is set in the system.
6. The system has access to order status and rider availability data.
TC-148.title: Assess interaction of delayed delivery penalty with no rider availability

steps.step: Create a test order that is running late (delivery_time_exceeded).
steps.result: The order is created successfully and marked as running late in the system.

steps.step: Ensure there are no available riders in the system.
steps.result: The system shows zero available riders.

steps.step: Trigger the Rider Handover Time (RHT) calculation for the late order.
steps.result: The system initiates the RHT calculation process.

steps.step: Verify that the system applies the delivery_time_exceeded penalty.
steps.result: The delivery_time_exceeded penalty is added to the RHT calculation.

steps.step: Check if the system recognizes that there are no available riders.
steps.result: The system correctly identifies that there are no available riders and applies the maximum handover time of 1800s.

steps.step: Examine the final Rider Handover Time calculation.
steps.result: The final RHT should be the sum of the delivery_time_exceeded penalty and the 1800s maximum time, but not exceeding any system-defined upper limit.

steps.step: Verify that the calculated RHT does not exceed the maximum allowed time in the system.
steps.result: The calculated RHT is within the system's maximum allowed time limit.

steps.step: Check the impact of the calculated RHT on the overall ETA calculation.
steps.result: The system correctly incorporates the calculated RHT into the overall ETA formula: Pure ride time (C) + max(Pure pick time (A) + Pick delay (B), Rider handover time (D)) + Rain buffer (F).

steps.step: Simulate the arrival of an available rider and recalculate the RHT.
steps.result: The system detects the newly available rider and recalculates the RHT, which should now be lower than the previous calculation.

steps.step: Compare the new RHT with the previous calculation.
steps.result: The new RHT is lower than the previous calculation, demonstrating that the system responds correctly to changes in rider availability.

TC-137.description: Confirm that the rider handover time is set to 1 second when there are more riders available than pending orders. Set number of riders to 10 and pending orders to 5.
TC-137.preconditions:
1. The order delivery system is up and running.
2. There are 10 riders available in the system.
3. There are 5 pending orders in the system.
4. The Rider Handover Time (RHT) calculation module is functional.
TC-137.title: Verify minimum handover time with excess riders

steps.step: Set up the test environment with 10 available riders and 5 pending orders.
steps.result: The system should recognize 10 riders and 5 pending orders.

steps.step: Trigger the Rider Handover Time calculation.
steps.result: The system should initiate the RHT calculation process.

steps.step: Observe the calculated Rider Handover Time.
steps.result: The Rider Handover Time should be set to 1 second.

steps.step: Verify the reasoning behind the 1-second handover time.
steps.result: The system should indicate that the minimum handover time is applied due to excess rider availability.

steps.step: Check if the handover time affects the overall ETA calculation.
steps.result: The overall ETA calculation should use the 1-second handover time in its formula: C + max(A+B, 1) + F, where 1 represents the handover time.

TC-138.description: Calculate handover time when the number of riders equals the number of pending orders. Set both riders and pending orders to 5, with varying remaining ride times. Verify that the handover time is correctly calculated using the heap of remaining ride times.
TC-138.preconditions:
1. The order delivery system is operational.
2. There are 5 riders available in the system.
3. There are 5 pending orders in the system.
4. Each rider has a different remaining ride time assigned.
5. The system's heap for remaining ride times is functional.
TC-138.title: Validate handover time calculation with equal riders and orders

steps.step: Set up the test environment with 5 available riders and 5 pending orders.
steps.result: The system should recognize 5 riders and 5 pending orders.

steps.step: Assign varying remaining ride times to each rider (e.g., 300s, 450s, 200s, 600s, 150s).
steps.result: Each rider should have a unique remaining ride time assigned.

steps.step: Trigger the Rider Handover Time calculation.
steps.result: The system should initiate the RHT calculation process using the heap of remaining ride times.

steps.step: Observe the calculated Rider Handover Time.
steps.result: The Rider Handover Time should be equal to the smallest remaining ride time in the heap (150s in this example).

steps.step: Verify the heap operation during the calculation.
steps.result: The system should have used the heap to efficiently find the minimum remaining ride time.

steps.step: Check if the handover time affects the overall ETA calculation.
steps.result: The overall ETA calculation should use the calculated handover time (150s in this example) in its formula: C + max(A+B, 150) + F.

TC-139.description: Compute handover time when there are fewer riders than pending orders. Set riders to 3 and pending orders to 5, with varying remaining ride times. Ensure the handover time is accurately calculated using the heap method.
TC-139.preconditions:
1. The order delivery system is fully functional.
2. There are 3 riders available in the system.
3. There are 5 pending orders in the system.
4. Each rider has a different remaining ride time assigned.
5. The system's heap for remaining ride times is operational.
TC-139.title: Test handover time with fewer riders than orders

steps.step: Set up the test environment with 3 available riders and 5 pending orders.
steps.result: The system should recognize 3 riders and 5 pending orders.

steps.step: Assign varying remaining ride times to each rider (e.g., 400s, 300s, 500s).
steps.result: Each rider should have a unique remaining ride time assigned.

steps.step: Trigger the Rider Handover Time calculation.
steps.result: The system should initiate the RHT calculation process using the heap of remaining ride times.

steps.step: Observe the calculated Rider Handover Time.
steps.result: The Rider Handover Time should be equal to the sum of the two smallest remaining ride times (300s + 400s = 700s in this example).

steps.step: Verify the heap operation during the calculation.
steps.result: The system should have used the heap to efficiently find and sum the two smallest remaining ride times.

steps.step: Check if the handover time affects the overall ETA calculation.
steps.result: The overall ETA calculation should use the calculated handover time (700s in this example) in its formula: C + max(A+B, 700) + F.

steps.step: Simulate the completion of an order and recalculate the handover time.
steps.result: The handover time should update to reflect the new situation with 3 riders and 4 pending orders.

TC-140.description: Verify that the maximum handover time of 1800 seconds (30 minutes) is applied when there are no riders available. Set number of riders to 0 and pending orders to any positive number.
TC-140.preconditions:
1. The order delivery system is operational.
2. There are 0 riders available in the system.
3. There is at least 1 pending order in the system (e.g., 5 pending orders).
4. The Rider Handover Time (RHT) calculation module is functional.
TC-140.title: Assess maximum handover time with no available riders

steps.step: Set up the test environment with 0 available riders and 5 pending orders.
steps.result: The system should recognize 0 riders and 5 pending orders.

steps.step: Trigger the Rider Handover Time calculation.
steps.result: The system should initiate the RHT calculation process.

steps.step: Observe the calculated Rider Handover Time.
steps.result: The Rider Handover Time should be set to the maximum value of 1800 seconds (30 minutes).

steps.step: Verify the reasoning behind the 1800-second handover time.
steps.result: The system should indicate that the maximum handover time is applied due to no available riders.

steps.step: Check if the handover time affects the overall ETA calculation.
steps.result: The overall ETA calculation should use the 1800-second handover time in its formula: C + max(A+B, 1800) + F.

steps.step: Simulate the arrival of a rider and recalculate the handover time.
steps.result: The handover time should update to reflect the new situation with 1 rider available.

TC-141.description: Test the system's handling of fractional seconds in remaining ride times. Create a scenario with riders and orders, using fractional remaining ride times. Verify correct calculation and rounding of the final handover time.
TC-141.preconditions:
1. The order delivery system is fully operational.
2. There are at least 3 riders available in the system.
3. There are at least 3 pending orders in the system.
4. The system's heap for remaining ride times is functional and can handle fractional seconds.
5. The Rider Handover Time (RHT) calculation module can process fractional seconds.
TC-141.title: Evaluate handover time calculation with fractional remaining ride times

steps.step: Set up the test environment with 3 available riders and 3 pending orders.
steps.result: The system should recognize 3 riders and 3 pending orders.

steps.step: Assign fractional remaining ride times to each rider (e.g., 300.5s, 450.75s, 200.25s).
steps.result: Each rider should have a unique fractional remaining ride time assigned.

steps.step: Trigger the Rider Handover Time calculation.
steps.result: The system should initiate the RHT calculation process using the heap of fractional remaining ride times.

steps.step: Observe the calculated Rider Handover Time.
steps.result: The Rider Handover Time should be equal to the smallest fractional remaining ride time in the heap (200.25s in this example).

steps.step: Verify the heap operation during the calculation.
steps.result: The system should have used the heap to efficiently find the minimum fractional remaining ride time.

steps.step: Check the precision of the calculated handover time.
steps.result: The handover time should be stored and displayed with the correct fractional precision (e.g., 200.25 seconds).

steps.step: Verify how the system rounds the fractional handover time for the overall ETA calculation.
steps.result: The system should use a consistent rounding method (e.g., round to nearest second or maintain fractional precision) when using the handover time in the ETA formula: C + max(A+B, 200.25) + F.

steps.step: Simulate the completion of an order and recalculate the handover time with new fractional values.
steps.result: The handover time should update correctly, maintaining fractional precision in the calculation.

TC-142.description: Assess how the system recalculates handover time when rider availability changes dynamically. Start with fewer riders than orders, then increase the number of riders. Verify that the handover time is updated correctly as more riders become available.
TC-142.preconditions:
1. The order delivery system is up and running.
2. There are multiple orders in the system ready for handover.
3. The initial number of available riders is less than the number of orders.
4. The system is configured to calculate Rider Handover Time (RHT) based on rider availability.
5. There is a mechanism to dynamically update rider availability in the system.
6. The system has the ability to recalculate RHT in real-time as rider availability changes.
TC-142.title: Check handover time updates with dynamic rider availability

steps.step: Set up the initial scenario with fewer riders than orders (e.g., 3 riders for 5 orders).
steps.result: The system should recognize that there are fewer riders than orders.

steps.step: Calculate the initial Rider Handover Time (RHT) for all orders.
steps.result: The system should calculate RHT using the heap of remaining ride time for equal/fewer riders. The RHT should be greater than 1 second for each order.

steps.step: Record the initial RHT for each order.
steps.result: The initial RHT values are stored for comparison.

steps.step: Increase the number of available riders to match the number of orders (e.g., add 2 more riders to make it 5 riders for 5 orders).
steps.result: The system should detect the change in rider availability.

steps.step: Trigger the recalculation of RHT for all orders.
steps.result: The system should recalculate RHT for all orders based on the new rider availability.

steps.step: Compare the new RHT values with the initial values.
steps.result: The new RHT values should be lower than the initial values, reflecting the increased rider availability. For orders that now have a dedicated rider, the RHT should be close to 1 second.

steps.step: Further increase the number of riders to exceed the number of orders (e.g., add 2 more riders to make it 7 riders for 5 orders).
steps.result: The system should detect the change in rider availability, with more riders than orders.

steps.step: Trigger another recalculation of RHT for all orders.
steps.result: The system should recalculate RHT for all orders. All orders should now have an RHT of 1 second, as there are more riders than orders.

steps.step: Verify that the RHT updates are reflected in the overall ETA calculation for each order.
steps.result: The overall ETA for each order should be updated to reflect the changes in RHT. The formula: Pure ride time (C) + max(Pure pick time (A) + Pick delay (B), Rider handover time (D)) + Rain buffer (F) should use the updated RHT values.

steps.step: Simulate a scenario where one rider becomes unavailable (e.g., marked out or offline).
steps.result: The system should detect the change and update the rider availability.

steps.step: Trigger a final recalculation of RHT for all orders.
steps.result: The system should recalculate RHT. Most orders should still have an RHT of 1 second, but one order might have a slightly higher RHT if the number of riders now equals the number of orders.

TC-131.description: Calculate pure ride time using a successful Google API response. Provide a sample API time of 600 seconds and consumer_del_factor of 1.1. Expected pure ride time should be 600s * 1.1 = 660s.
TC-131.preconditions:
1. The order delivery system is up and running.
2. Google API is accessible and functioning correctly.
3. The consumer_del_factor is set to 1.1.
4. A test order is created in the system.
TC-131.title: Verify ride time calculation using Google API

steps.step: Set up a mock Google API response with a travel time of 600 seconds.
steps.result: The mock API is set up successfully.

steps.step: Trigger the Pure Ride Time calculation for the test order.

steps.result: The Pure Ride Time of 660 seconds is accurately stored and associated with the test order.

TC-132.description: Confirm that the system uses the default_delivery_travel_time of 15 minutes (900 seconds) when the Google API call fails. Simulate an API failure and verify the fallback behavior.
TC-132.preconditions:

2. The default_delivery_travel_time is configured as 15 minutes (900 seconds).
3. A test order is created in the system.

steps.step: Trigger the Pure Ride Time calculation for the test order.
steps.result: The system initiates the Pure Ride Time calculation.

steps.step: Verify that the system attempts to call the Google API.
steps.result: The system calls the Google API, which returns a failure response as expected.

steps.step: Check the calculated Pure Ride Time.
steps.result: The system correctly falls back to the default_delivery_travel_time of 900 seconds (15 minutes).

steps.step: Confirm that the fallback Pure Ride Time is stored correctly in the order details.
steps.result: The Pure Ride Time of 900 seconds is accurately stored and associated with the test order.

steps.step: Verify that the system logs the API failure appropriately.
steps.result: The API failure is logged in the system for debugging and monitoring purposes.

TC-133.description: Calculate pure ride time using the minimum possible time returned by the Google API (likely 1 second). Apply the consumer_del_factor and verify correct handling of very short delivery times.

2. Google API is accessible and functioning correctly.
3. The consumer_del_factor is set to 1.1.
4. A test order is created in the system for a very short distance delivery.

steps.result: The mock API is set up successfully with a 1-second response.

steps.step: Trigger the Pure Ride Time calculation for the test order.
steps.result: The system initiates the Pure Ride Time calculation.

steps.step: Verify that the system calls the Google API to get the travel time.
steps.result: The system successfully calls the Google API and receives the mocked response of 1 second.

steps.step: Check the calculated Pure Ride Time.

steps.step: Verify how the system handles the fractional second result.

steps.step: Confirm that the calculated Pure Ride Time is stored correctly in the order details.
steps.result: The Pure Ride Time (either 1.1 seconds or the rounded value) is accurately stored and associated with the test order.

TC-134.description: Compute pure ride time using an extremely large time value from the Google API (e.g., 2 hours or 7200 seconds). Apply the consumer_del_factor and ensure the system correctly handles long delivery times.
TC-134.preconditions:

steps.step: Confirm that the calculated Pure Ride Time is stored correctly in the order details.
steps.result: The Pure Ride Time of 7920 seconds is accurately stored and associated with the test order.

steps.step: Check if the system triggers any alerts or notifications for unusually long delivery times.
steps.result: The system may or may not trigger alerts based on the configured thresholds for long delivery times.

TC-135.description: Verify the system's handling of fractional seconds in the Google API response. Use an API time of 652.7 seconds with the consumer_del_factor. Confirm correct calculation and rounding of the final pure ride time.
TC-135.preconditions:

2. Google API is accessible and functioning correctly.
3. The consumer_del_factor is set to 1.1.

steps.step: Trigger the Pure Ride Time calculation for the test order.

steps.step: Check the calculated Pure Ride Time.

steps.step: Verify how the system handles the fractional second result.

TC-136.description: Test the system's ability to use a different consumer_del_factor. Change the factor to 1.2 and verify that the pure ride time is correctly calculated using this new value.
TC-136.preconditions:
1. The system is set up and configured for ETA calculation.
2. The Google API for calculating travel time is functional and accessible.
3. The consumer_del_factor is initially set to 1.1.
4. An order is in the system ready for ETA calculation.
5. Access to modify system configuration parameters is available.
TC-136.title: Evaluate ride time calculation with modified consumer delivery factor

steps.step: Change the consumer_del_factor in the system configuration from 1.1 to 1.2.
steps.result: The system accepts the new consumer_del_factor value of 1.2.

steps.step: Trigger the Pure Ride Time (PRT) calculation for the order.
steps.result: The system initiates the PRT calculation process.

steps.step: Verify that the system successfully calls the Google API to get the travel time.
steps.result: The Google API returns a travel time value (e.g., 1000 seconds).

steps.step: Check the calculated Pure Ride Time.
steps.result: The Pure Ride Time is correctly calculated as: Google API time * 1.2 (new consumer_del_factor). For example, if Google API returns 1000 seconds, PRT should be 1200 seconds.

steps.step: Compare the current PRT calculation with a previous calculation using the old consumer_del_factor (1.1).
steps.result: The new PRT value is proportionally higher than the old value, reflecting the increase in consumer_del_factor from 1.1 to 1.2.

steps.step: Attempt to calculate PRT for multiple orders with varying distances.
steps.result: All PRTs are consistently calculated using the new consumer_del_factor of 1.2.

steps.step: Revert the consumer_del_factor back to 1.1.
steps.result: The system accepts the change and reverts to using 1.1 as the consumer_del_factor.

steps.step: Recalculate PRT for the same order.
steps.result: The PRT is now calculated using the original 1.1 factor, resulting in a proportionally lower value compared to step 4.

TC-125.description: Confirm that an additional 300 seconds delay is added when an order is running delayed. Create an order that has exceeded its expected pick time and verify that the 300s delay is correctly applied to the pick delay calculation.
TC-125.preconditions:
1. The order delivery system is up and running.
2. There is at least one picker available in the system.
3. An order has been created in the system.
4. The order's expected pick time has been exceeded.
TC-125.title: Verify additional delay for running delayed orders

steps.step: Create an order in the system with a specific expected pick time.
steps.result: The order is successfully created with the specified expected pick time.

steps.step: Allow the order's expected pick time to elapse without the order being picked.
steps.result: The order's status changes to 'running delayed'.

steps.step: Trigger the pick delay calculation for this order.
steps.result: The system calculates the pick delay for the order.

steps.step: Check the pick delay calculation details.
steps.result: The pick delay calculation includes an additional 300 seconds delay for the running delayed order.

steps.step: Verify the total pick delay time.
steps.result: The total pick delay time is equal to the standard pick delay (based on picker availability) plus 300 seconds.

TC-126.description: Ensure that no additional delay is added for orders that are not running late. Create an order within its expected pick time and verify that only the standard pick delay (based on picker availability) is applied without the 300s penalty.
TC-126.preconditions:
1. The order delivery system is up and running.
2. There is at least one picker available in the system.
3. An order has been created in the system.
4. The order is within its expected pick time.
TC-126.title: Validate no additional delay for on-time orders

steps.step: Create an order in the system with a specific expected pick time.
steps.result: The order is successfully created with the specified expected pick time.

steps.step: Ensure the current time is within the order's expected pick time.
steps.result: The order's status is 'on-time'.

steps.step: Trigger the pick delay calculation for this order.
steps.result: The system calculates the pick delay for the order.

steps.step: Check the pick delay calculation details.
steps.result: The pick delay calculation does not include any additional delay beyond the standard pick delay based on picker availability.

steps.step: Verify the total pick delay time.
steps.result: The total pick delay time is equal to the standard pick delay (based on picker availability) without any additional 300 seconds penalty.

TC-127.description: Assess how the system handles multiple delayed orders. Create several orders that are running late and verify that the 300s delay is applied correctly to each, potentially resulting in a cumulative delay in the pick delay calculation.
TC-127.preconditions:
1. The order delivery system is up and running.
2. There are multiple pickers available in the system.
3. Multiple orders have been created in the system.
4. Some of these orders have exceeded their expected pick times.
TC-127.title: Test cumulative delay for multiple delayed orders

steps.step: Create multiple orders in the system with specific expected pick times.
steps.result: Multiple orders are successfully created with specified expected pick times.

steps.step: Allow some of the orders' expected pick times to elapse without being picked.
steps.result: The status of these orders changes to 'running delayed'.

steps.step: Trigger the pick delay calculation for all orders.
steps.result: The system calculates the pick delay for all orders.

steps.step: Check the pick delay calculation details for each delayed order.
steps.result: Each delayed order's pick delay calculation includes an additional 300 seconds delay.

steps.step: Verify the total pick delay time for all orders.
steps.result: The total pick delay time for each delayed order includes the 300 seconds penalty, potentially resulting in a cumulative delay across all orders.

steps.step: Compare the pick delay of delayed orders with non-delayed orders.
steps.result: Delayed orders have an additional 300 seconds in their pick delay compared to non-delayed orders.

TC-128.description: Evaluate the system's behavior when an order transitions from on-time to delayed. Create an order that is exactly at the threshold of its expected pick time and observe how the system applies the delay as it crosses into 'delayed' status.
TC-128.preconditions:
1. The order delivery system is up and running.
2. There is at least one picker available in the system.
3. An order has been created in the system.
4. The order is approaching its expected pick time threshold.
TC-128.title: Check delay application at the threshold of becoming delayed

steps.step: Create an order in the system with a specific expected pick time close to the current time.
steps.result: The order is successfully created with the specified expected pick time.

steps.step: Monitor the order status as it approaches the expected pick time.
steps.result: The order status remains 'on-time' until it reaches the expected pick time.

steps.step: Allow the order to cross the expected pick time threshold.
steps.result: The order status changes from 'on-time' to 'running delayed' as it crosses the threshold.

steps.step: Trigger the pick delay calculation immediately before the order becomes delayed.
steps.result: The pick delay calculation does not include the 300 seconds penalty.

steps.step: Trigger the pick delay calculation immediately after the order becomes delayed.
steps.result: The pick delay calculation now includes the 300 seconds penalty.

steps.step: Compare the two pick delay calculations.
steps.result: The pick delay calculation after crossing the threshold is exactly 300 seconds more than the calculation before crossing the threshold.

TC-129.description: Test the system's ability to remove the additional delay when a previously delayed order becomes on-time (e.g., due to updated ETA calculations or changes in picker availability). Ensure the 300s penalty is removed appropriately.
TC-129.preconditions:
1. The order delivery system is up and running.
2. There are multiple pickers available in the system.
3. An order has been created and is currently in a 'running delayed' status.
4. The system allows for updates to ETA calculations or changes in picker availability.
TC-129.title: Verify delay removal when delayed order becomes on-time

steps.step: Create an order in the system that is currently in a 'running delayed' status.
steps.result: The order is successfully created and its status is 'running delayed'.

steps.step: Verify that the current pick delay calculation includes the 300 seconds penalty.
steps.result: The pick delay calculation includes the 300 seconds penalty for the delayed order.

steps.step: Update the system conditions to bring the order back to 'on-time' status (e.g., update ETA calculations or increase picker availability).
steps.result: The system conditions are updated successfully.

steps.step: Trigger a recalculation of the order's status.
steps.result: The order's status changes from 'running delayed' to 'on-time'.

steps.step: Trigger a new pick delay calculation for the order.
steps.result: The system performs a new pick delay calculation for the order.

steps.step: Check the new pick delay calculation details.
steps.result: The new pick delay calculation does not include the 300 seconds penalty.

steps.step: Compare the new pick delay with the previous pick delay.
steps.result: The new pick delay is exactly 300 seconds less than the previous pick delay, confirming that the penalty has been removed.

TC-130.description: Evaluate how the system combines the 300s delay for running late orders with the maximum delay of 1800s when no pickers are available. Verify that the total delay does not exceed the maximum allowed delay in the system.
TC-130.preconditions:
1. The system is configured with a 300s delay penalty for running delayed orders.
2. The system is configured with a maximum delay of 1800s when no pickers are available.
3. There is at least one order in the system that is running delayed.
4. There are currently no pickers available at the store.
TC-130.title: Assess interaction of delayed order penalty with no picker availability

steps.step: Create a delayed order in the system
steps.result: The order is successfully created and marked as delayed

steps.step: Ensure that there are no pickers available at the store
steps.result: The system confirms that there are no available pickers

steps.step: Trigger the Pick Delay (PD) calculation for the delayed order
steps.result: The system initiates the Pick Delay calculation

steps.step: Verify the Pick Delay value calculated by the system
steps.result: The Pick Delay value should be 1800s (30 minutes), which is the maximum delay when no pickers are available. The system should not add the 300s delay for running late orders on top of this maximum value.

steps.step: Add a picker to the store and mark them as available
steps.result: A picker is successfully added and marked as available in the system

steps.step: Trigger the Pick Delay (PD) calculation again for the same delayed order
steps.result: The system recalculates the Pick Delay

steps.step: Verify the new Pick Delay value
steps.result: The new Pick Delay value should be 300s (5 minutes), which is the penalty for running delayed orders. The maximum delay of 1800s should no longer apply as there is now an available picker.

steps.step: Create multiple delayed orders and calculate Pick Delay with varying picker availability
steps.result: The system correctly applies the 300s delay for each delayed order when pickers are available, and caps the delay at 1800s when no pickers are available, never exceeding this maximum value.

TC-119.description: Confirm that pick delay is 0 when there are more pickers than pending orders. Set number of pickers to 10 and pending orders to 5. Expected pick delay should be 0 seconds.
TC-119.preconditions:
1. The order delivery system is up and running.
2. The Pick Delay (PD) calculation module is functional.
3. There are 10 pickers available at the store.
4. There are 5 pending orders in the system.
5. All pickers are marked as 'in' and ready to pick orders.
TC-119.title: Verify zero pick delay with excess pickers

steps.step: Set the number of available pickers to 10.
steps.result: The system successfully registers 10 available pickers.

steps.step: Set the number of pending orders to 5.
steps.result: The system successfully registers 5 pending orders.

steps.step: Trigger the Pick Delay calculation for the current scenario.
steps.result: The system initiates the Pick Delay calculation process.

steps.step: Retrieve the calculated Pick Delay value.
steps.result: The system returns a Pick Delay value of 0 seconds.

steps.step: Verify that the Pick Delay is exactly 0 seconds.
steps.result: The Pick Delay is confirmed to be 0 seconds, indicating no delay when there are more pickers than pending orders.

TC-120.description: Calculate pick delay when the number of pickers equals the number of pending orders. Set both pickers and pending orders to 5, with varying remaining pick times. Verify that the pick delay is correctly calculated using the heap of remaining pick times.
TC-120.preconditions:
1. The order delivery system is operational.
2. The Pick Delay (PD) calculation module is functioning correctly.
3. There are 5 pickers available at the store.
4. There are 5 pending orders in the system.
5. All pickers are marked as 'in' and ready to pick orders.
6. The system is configured to use a heap of remaining pick times for PD calculation when pickers and orders are equal.
TC-120.title: Validate pick delay calculation with equal pickers and orders

steps.step: Set the number of available pickers to 5.
steps.result: The system successfully registers 5 available pickers.

steps.step: Set the number of pending orders to 5.
steps.result: The system successfully registers 5 pending orders.

steps.step: Assign varying remaining pick times to the 5 orders: 100s, 150s, 200s, 250s, 300s.
steps.result: The system successfully assigns the specified remaining pick times to the orders.

steps.step: Trigger the Pick Delay calculation for the current scenario.
steps.result: The system initiates the Pick Delay calculation process using the heap of remaining pick times.

steps.step: Retrieve the calculated Pick Delay value.
steps.result: The system returns a Pick Delay value based on the heap calculation.

steps.step: Verify that the Pick Delay is correctly calculated using the heap method.
steps.result: The Pick Delay is confirmed to be the sum of the top 'n' values in the heap, where 'n' is the number of pickers (5 in this case).

steps.step: Compare the calculated Pick Delay with the expected value (sum of 100s, 150s, 200s, 250s, 300s).
steps.result: The calculated Pick Delay matches the expected value of 1000 seconds.

TC-121.description: Compute pick delay when there are fewer pickers than pending orders. Set pickers to 3 and pending orders to 5, with varying remaining pick times. Ensure the pick delay is accurately calculated using the heap method.
TC-121.preconditions:
1. The order delivery system is operational.
2. The Pick Delay (PD) calculation module is functioning correctly.
3. There are 3 pickers available at the store.
4. There are 5 pending orders in the system.
5. All pickers are marked as 'in' and ready to pick orders.
6. The system is configured to use a heap of remaining pick times for PD calculation when there are fewer pickers than orders.
TC-121.title: Test pick delay with fewer pickers than orders

steps.step: Set the number of available pickers to 3.
steps.result: The system successfully registers 3 available pickers.

steps.step: Set the number of pending orders to 5.
steps.result: The system successfully registers 5 pending orders.

steps.step: Assign varying remaining pick times to the 5 orders: 100s, 150s, 200s, 250s, 300s.
steps.result: The system successfully assigns the specified remaining pick times to the orders.

steps.step: Trigger the Pick Delay calculation for the current scenario.
steps.result: The system initiates the Pick Delay calculation process using the heap of remaining pick times.

steps.step: Retrieve the calculated Pick Delay value.
steps.result: The system returns a Pick Delay value based on the heap calculation.

steps.step: Verify that the Pick Delay is correctly calculated using the heap method.
steps.result: The Pick Delay is confirmed to be the sum of the top 'n' values in the heap, where 'n' is the number of pickers (3 in this case).

steps.step: Compare the calculated Pick Delay with the expected value (sum of 200s, 250s, 300s).
steps.result: The calculated Pick Delay matches the expected value of 750 seconds.

TC-122.description: Verify that the maximum pick delay of 1800 seconds (30 minutes) is applied when there are no pickers available at the store. Set number of pickers to 0 and pending orders to any positive number.
TC-122.preconditions:
1. The order delivery system is operational.
2. The Pick Delay (PD) calculation module is functioning correctly.
3. There are 0 pickers available at the store.
4. There is at least 1 pending order in the system.
5. The system is configured to apply a maximum pick delay of 1800 seconds when no pickers are available.
TC-122.title: Assess maximum pick delay with no available pickers

steps.step: Set the number of available pickers to 0.
steps.result: The system successfully registers 0 available pickers.

steps.step: Set the number of pending orders to 3 (or any positive number).
steps.result: The system successfully registers 3 pending orders.

steps.step: Trigger the Pick Delay calculation for the current scenario.
steps.result: The system initiates the Pick Delay calculation process.

steps.step: Retrieve the calculated Pick Delay value.
steps.result: The system returns a Pick Delay value.

steps.step: Verify that the Pick Delay is exactly 1800 seconds (30 minutes).
steps.result: The Pick Delay is confirmed to be 1800 seconds, indicating the maximum delay when no pickers are available.

steps.step: Change the number of pending orders to a different positive number (e.g., 10) and recalculate the Pick Delay.
steps.result: The system recalculates and still returns a Pick Delay of 1800 seconds, regardless of the number of pending orders.

TC-123.description: Test the system's handling of fractional seconds in remaining pick times. Create a scenario with pickers and orders, using fractional remaining pick times. Verify correct calculation and rounding of the final pick delay.
TC-123.preconditions:
1. The order delivery system is operational.
2. The Pick Delay (PD) calculation module is functioning correctly.
3. The system is capable of handling fractional seconds in remaining pick times.
4. There are at least 3 pickers available at the store.
5. There are at least 3 pending orders in the system.
6. All pickers are marked as 'in' and ready to pick orders.
TC-123.title: Evaluate pick delay calculation with fractional remaining pick times

steps.step: Set the number of available pickers to 3.
steps.result: The system successfully registers 3 available pickers.

steps.step: Set the number of pending orders to 3.
steps.result: The system successfully registers 3 pending orders.

steps.step: Assign fractional remaining pick times to the 3 orders: 100.5s, 150.7s, 200.2s.
steps.result: The system successfully assigns the specified fractional remaining pick times to the orders.

steps.step: Trigger the Pick Delay calculation for the current scenario.
steps.result: The system initiates the Pick Delay calculation process using the heap of remaining pick times.

steps.step: Retrieve the calculated Pick Delay value.
steps.result: The system returns a Pick Delay value based on the heap calculation.

steps.step: Verify that the Pick Delay is correctly calculated using the heap method, accounting for fractional seconds.
steps.result: The Pick Delay is confirmed to be the sum of all three fractional values: 451.4 seconds.

steps.step: Check if the system rounds the final Pick Delay value.
steps.result: The system either maintains the fractional value of 451.4 seconds or rounds it according to the defined rounding rules (e.g., to 451 or 452 seconds).

steps.step: Verify that the Pick Delay calculation handles fractional seconds consistently across multiple calculations.
steps.result: Repeated calculations with the same input consistently produce the same result, demonstrating reliable handling of fractional seconds.

TC-124.description: Assess how the system recalculates pick delay when picker availability changes dynamically. Start with fewer pickers than orders, then increase the number of pickers. Verify that the pick delay is updated correctly as more pickers become available.
TC-124.preconditions:
1. The order delivery system is up and running.
2. There are multiple pending orders in the system.
3. The system has a mechanism to track and update picker availability in real-time.
4. The Pick Delay (PD) calculation module is functional.
5. Initial state: There are fewer pickers than pending orders.
TC-124.title: Check pick delay updates with dynamic picker availability

steps.step: Record the initial number of pickers and pending orders.
steps.result: The system accurately records the initial state with fewer pickers than pending orders.

steps.step: Calculate and note the initial Pick Delay (PD) for a specific order.
steps.result: The system calculates PD using the heap of remaining pick time, as there are fewer pickers than orders. The PD value is positive and reflects the current picker shortage.

steps.step: Increase the number of available pickers gradually, ensuring it's still less than the number of pending orders.
steps.result: The system detects the change in picker availability and updates its internal state.

steps.step: Recalculate the Pick Delay for the same order.
steps.result: The PD value decreases compared to the initial calculation, reflecting the increased picker availability. The system still uses the heap of remaining pick time for calculation.

steps.step: Continue increasing the number of pickers until it equals the number of pending orders.
steps.result: The system recognizes that the number of pickers now equals the number of pending orders.

steps.step: Recalculate the Pick Delay once more.
steps.result: The PD value further decreases, potentially approaching zero, as there are now enough pickers to handle all pending orders.

steps.step: Increase the number of pickers to exceed the number of pending orders.
steps.result: The system detects that there are now more pickers than pending orders.

steps.step: Perform a final Pick Delay calculation.
steps.result: The PD value should now be zero or minimal, as there are more than enough pickers to handle all orders without delay.

steps.step: Verify that the system didn't add any additional delay penalties (like the 300s for delayed orders or 1800s for no pickers) during this process.
steps.result: The system correctly avoided applying unnecessary delay penalties as pickers were always available, just in varying numbers.

steps.step: Check the system logs or monitoring tools to ensure that Pick Delay calculations were performed and updated in real-time as picker availability changed.
steps.result: The logs or monitoring tools show that PD calculations were performed and updated promptly with each change in picker availability.

TC-114.description: Calculate the total pick buffer time by combining extra items buffer and loose items buffer. Set num_skus to 15, max_items_for_picking to 10, extra_items_buffer to 40s, loose_item_count to 5, and loose_item_buffer to 30s. Expect a total buffer of (5 * 40s) + (5 * 30s) = 350s.
TC-114.preconditions:
1. The system is configured with the following parameters:
   - default_picking_time: 200s
   - max_items_for_picking: 10
   - extra_items_buffer: 40s
   - loose_item_buffer: 30s
2. An order is created with the following details:
   - num_skus: 15
   - loose_item_count: 5
3. The Pure Pick Time (PPT) calculation feature is enabled and functioning correctly.
TC-114.title: Verify combined pick buffer calculation with extra items and loose items

steps.step: Calculate the extra items buffer
steps.result:
The system should calculate the extra items buffer as:
(num_skus - max_items_for_picking) * extra_items_buffer
= (15 - 10) * 40s = 5 * 40s = 200s

steps.step: Calculate the loose items buffer
steps.result:
The system should calculate the loose items buffer as:
loose_item_count * loose_item_buffer
= 5 * 30s = 150s

steps.step: Calculate the total picking buffer time
steps.result:
The system should calculate the total picking buffer time as:
extra items buffer + loose items buffer
= 200s + 150s = 350s

steps.step: Calculate the final Pure Pick Time (PPT)
steps.result:
The system should calculate the final PPT as:
default_picking_time + total picking buffer time
= 200s + 350s = 550s

steps.step: Verify the calculated Pure Pick Time
steps.result: The calculated Pure Pick Time should be 550s (9 minutes and 10 seconds)

TC-115.description: Calculate the pick buffer time when there are extra items but no loose items. Set num_skus to 12, max_items_for_picking to 10, extra_items_buffer to 40s, and loose_item_count to 0. Expect a buffer of (2 * 40s) = 80s.
TC-115.preconditions:
1. The system is configured with the following parameters:
   - default_picking_time: 200s
   - max_items_for_picking: 10
   - extra_items_buffer: 40s
2. An order is created with the following details:
   - num_skus: 12
   - loose_item_count: 0
3. The Pure Pick Time (PPT) calculation feature is enabled and functioning correctly.
TC-115.title: Validate pick buffer calculation with only extra items

steps.step: Calculate the extra items buffer
steps.result:
The system should calculate the extra items buffer as:
(num_skus - max_items_for_picking) * extra_items_buffer
= (12 - 10) * 40s = 2 * 40s = 80s

steps.step: Calculate the loose items buffer
steps.result:
The system should calculate the loose items buffer as:
loose_item_count * loose_item_buffer
= 0 * 30s = 0s

steps.step: Calculate the total picking buffer time
steps.result:
The system should calculate the total picking buffer time as:
extra items buffer + loose items buffer
= 80s + 0s = 80s

steps.step: Calculate the final Pure Pick Time (PPT)
steps.result:
The system should calculate the final PPT as:
default_picking_time + total picking buffer time
= 200s + 80s = 280s

steps.step: Verify the calculated Pure Pick Time
steps.result: The calculated Pure Pick Time should be 280s (4 minutes and 40 seconds)

TC-116.description: Verify the pick buffer time when there are loose items but no extra items. Set num_skus to 8 (below max_items_for_picking), loose_item_count to 3, and loose_item_buffer to 30s. Expect a buffer of (3 * 30s) = 90s.

steps.step: Calculate the loose items buffer
steps.result:

steps.step: Calculate the total picking buffer time

steps.step: Verify the calculated Pure Pick Time

TC-118.description: Test the system's handling of fractional seconds in buffer calculations. Set extra_items_buffer to 40.5s and loose_item_buffer to 30.5s, with 2 extra items and 3 loose items. Verify correct rounding or precision in the final buffer time.
TC-118.preconditions:
1. The system is configured with the following parameters:
   - default_picking_time: 200s
   - max_items_for_picking: 10
   - extra_items_buffer: 40.5s
   - loose_item_buffer: 30.5s
2. An order is created with the following details:
   - num_skus: 12 (2 extra items)
   - loose_item_count: 3
3. The Pure Pick Time (PPT) calculation feature is enabled and functioning correctly.
4. The system is capable of handling fractional seconds in calculations.
TC-118.title: Evaluate pick buffer calculation with fractional seconds

steps.step: Calculate the extra items buffer
steps.result:
The system should calculate the extra items buffer as:
(num_skus - max_items_for_picking) * extra_items_buffer
= (12 - 10) * 40.5s = 2 * 40.5s = 81s

steps.step: Calculate the loose items buffer
steps.result:
The system should calculate the loose items buffer as:
loose_item_count * loose_item_buffer
= 3 * 30.5s = 91.5s

steps.step: Calculate the total picking buffer time
steps.result:
The system should calculate the total picking buffer time as:
extra items buffer + loose items buffer
= 81s + 91.5s = 172.5s

steps.step: Calculate the final Pure Pick Time (PPT)
steps.result:
The system should calculate the final PPT as:
default_picking_time + total picking buffer time
= 200s + 172.5s = 372.5s

steps.step: Verify the calculated Pure Pick Time
steps.result: The calculated Pure Pick Time should be 372.5s (6 minutes and 12.5 seconds)

steps.step: Check the system's handling of fractional seconds
steps.result: The system should either maintain the precision of 372.5s or round it to the nearest whole second (373s) based on the system's configuration. The handling of fractional seconds should be consistent across all calculations.

TC-103.description: Verify the system's ability to use a different default picking time. Change the default picking time configuration (e.g., to 180 seconds) and confirm that this new value is correctly used as the base pick time.
TC-103.preconditions:
1. The system allows modification of the default picking time configuration.
2. Access to change system configurations is available.
3. An order with a number of SKUs less than or equal to max_items_for_picking and no loose items can be created.
TC-103.title: Assess pick time calculation with modified default time

steps.step: Access the system configuration settings.
steps.result: The configuration settings are successfully accessed.

steps.step: Modify the default picking time from 200 seconds to 180 seconds.
steps.result: The default picking time is successfully changed to 180 seconds.

steps.step: Create an order with a number of SKUs equal to or less than max_items_for_picking and no loose items.
steps.result: The order is successfully created in the system.

steps.step: Trigger the Pure Pick Time (PPT) calculation for the created order.
steps.result: The system initiates the PPT calculation process with the new default picking time.

steps.step: Observe the calculated Pure Pick Time.
steps.result: The Pure Pick Time is exactly 180 seconds, reflecting the new default picking time.

steps.step: Verify that no additional buffer time has been added to the new base pick time.
steps.result: The system confirms that no extra buffer time was added, as the conditions for additional buffers were not met.

steps.step: Reset the default picking time back to 200 seconds.
steps.result: The default picking time is successfully reverted to 200 seconds to maintain system consistency for other tests.
